coredata.dc:description: Traditional drug administration routs suffer from low selective distribution, poor solubility, fast elimination and damaging adjacent healthy tissues (especially in cancer therapy). Thus, drug delivery systems (DDSs) which could deliver drug to target site have broadly been investigated to upsurge the drug bioavailability and to diminish its side effects. Nanocarriers can quickly internalize into cells through intracellular endocytic routes and efficiently release pharmaceuticals at desired place. Micro- and nanoparticle formulations are of great interest as materials for delivery of drugs. Microparticles can be utilized to prepare longstanding stock formulations that are stable for weeks to months and injected through intramuscular and/or subcutaneous methods. Nanocarriers are achieved from numerous inorganic and organic compounds such as polymers, dendrimers, lipids, proteins, antibodies, peptides, cells, metals and metal oxides (e.g. iron oxide, silver and gold), non-metal oxides (silica), quantum dots, carbon nanotubes and mesoporous materials. Assembly of these materials can produce several nanoscale architectures including microspheres, sheets, conjugates, micelles, vesicles, polymer nanoparticles, polyplexes and liposomes in which drug molecules are conjugated chemically or complexed/encapsulated physically. Stimuli responsive nanocarriers are delivery vehicles that are able to change their structures (such as degradation or decomposition), conformations or compositions to response internal and/or external biochemical and/or physical stimuli that help controlled release of encapsulated pharmaceuticals into diseased tissues. This review presents a comprehensive study on big family of nano- and micromaterials used as DDSs ranging from inorganic nanomaterials to polymeric nanocomposites and stimuli-responsive carriers as well as drug conjugates.

head.ce:title: Big family of nano- and microscale drug delivery systems ranging from inorganic materials to polymeric and stimuli-responsive carriers as well as drug-conjugates
head.ce:short-title: Nano- and microscale drug carriers as well as drug-conjugates

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional drug administration routs suffer from low selective distribution, poor solubility, fast elimination and damaging adjacent healthy tissues (especially in cancer therapy). Thus, drug delivery systems (DDSs) which could deliver drug to target site have broadly been investigated to upsurge the drug bioavailability and to diminish its side effects. Nanocarriers can quickly internalize into cells through intracellular endocytic routes and efficiently release pharmaceuticals at desired place. Micro- and nanoparticle formulations are of great interest as materials for delivery of drugs. Microparticles can be utilized to prepare longstanding stock formulations that are stable for weeks to months and injected through intramuscular and/or subcutaneous methods. Nanocarriers are achieved from numerous inorganic and organic compounds such as polymers, dendrimers, lipids, proteins, antibodies, peptides, cells, metals and metal oxides (e.g. iron oxide, silver and gold), non-metal oxides (silica), quantum dots, carbon nanotubes and mesoporous materials. Assembly of these materials can produce several nanoscale architectures including microspheres, sheets, conjugates, micelles, vesicles, polymer nanoparticles, polyplexes and liposomes in which drug molecules are conjugated chemically or complexed/encapsulated physically. Stimuli responsive nanocarriers are delivery vehicles that are able to change their structures (such as degradation or decomposition), conformations or compositions to response internal and/or external biochemical and/or physical stimuli that help controlled release of encapsulated pharmaceuticals into diseased tissues. This review presents a comprehensive study on big family of nano- and micromaterials used as DDSs ranging from inorganic nanomaterials to polymeric nanocomposites and stimuli-responsive carriers as well as drug conjugates.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the drug delivery has received great attention and numerous drug delivery systems (DDSs) have been prepared for site-specific drug targeting to pathological tissues [1–5]. The recent progress in drug delivery is mainly due to the development in polymer, chemistry, material science, biotechnology and nano-engineering that has led to the production of large number of various nanomaterials as promising drug delivery systems. Some clinically available and rather well established nanocarriers are polymers, liposomes, micelles, antibodies and proteins [6–10]. Such formulations are extensively used both in animal models and in patients and some of them have been approved for routine use by the responsible regulatory agencies [11–13]. Current drug carriers range in nature from peptides, nanobodies, viruses and cells to gold, iron oxide, silica/carbon nanoparticles, and in shape from spheres and sheets/shells to diamonds, tubes and cubes [14–16]. Moreover, pharmaceutical particles have various shapes and sizes which are changed from traditional granules and tablets to nano- and microparticles [17–21]. Tablets are the most famous formulations and granules are used to prepare tablets. The drug-incorporated granules are coated and/or mixed by medical polymers in order to achieve formulations with the sustained/delayed release ability [22,23]. The first DDSs that could release drugs in a sustained manner were fabricated in 1952 through coating the drugs as cores by various layers of a polymer [24]. The microparticle and nanoparticle formulations have recently been developed as drug delivery platforms. The microparticle carriers can be employed to afford durable formulations (that is, weeks to months) which are injected through intramuscular and/or subcutaneous methods. Usually, biodegradable polymers are utilized to develop long-term microparticle formulations and it is not necessary remove them after their lifetimes are ended (i.e., when their total loaded drugs are released). Nanoparticles are used to prepare formulations with special properties as unique delivery systems [25,26].

It is known that traditional drug administration route suffers from low selective distribution, poor solubility, damage to neighboring healthy tissues (particularly in cancer therapy) and fast drug elimination. Therefore, DDSs which are able to deliver drugs to target sites have broadly been used to increase the bioavailability and decrease the drugs side effects. Also, it has been found that nanocarriers can quickly internalize cells by the intracellular endocytosis paths and efficiently release medications at target positions. Consequently, numerous efforts are performed to achieve biocompatible, smart and cost-effective DDSs [27,28]. The drug nanocarriers are designed to overcome diseases either by therapeutics accumulation in diseased sites or through control of pathogens by pharmaceutical compounds [29–31]. Such nanocarriers are obtained using diverse inorganic and organic materials including polymers, dendrimers, lipids, mesoporous materials quantum dots and carbon nanotubes. Assemblies of these materials lead to the creation of several nanoscale architectures such as micelles, microspheres, vesicles, polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, polyplexes and conjugates to which active therapeutics that are conjugated chemically or complexed and encapsulated physically [32–35]. Nanocarriers reveal exceptional features to deliver therapeutic cargos like passage through biological barriers (like the blood brain barrier) or interactions by receptors existing over the cell surface [36–38]. The benefits of such DDSs are more drug accumulation at the pathological/diseased sites, long circulation time, higher cellular uptake, great body stability and decreased drugs toxic side effects on the normal tissues/cells [21–27].

The stimuli responsive nanocarriers have been developed as efficient smart delivery vehicles [35,39,40]. These nanocarriers are able to vary their compositions, structures or conformations to response internal and/or external biochemical or physical stimuli and lead to releasing entrapped active agents. The instant therapeutics release can be affected by structural variations in the nanocarrier (e.g., decomposition and degradation) but the linker degradation in the polymer-drug conjugates can result in the cargo release with the release rate mainly depends on the linker degradation rate [41]. Indeed, the smart DDSs can deliver a proper dose according to the patient requirement [42]. Currently, controlled drug delivery, targeted drug delivery and triggered drug release are widely studied. For example, some systems are able to interact with intracellular components responding to environmental stimuli or several nanoparticles can specifically attach the tumor cells via receptor targeted groups [43]. Also, the stimulated release can significantly control location and period of medication release because it is induced externally through various stimuli which influence the response of intracellular vehicles. Some stimuli applied to expedite drug release include pH, temperature, electric and magnetic fields, ultrasound, light and enzymatic activity [44].

It is noteworthy that the Biopharmaceutical Classification System has found that >40% of the drugs are not (or slightly) water soluble so that 90% of drugs that are approved from 1995 are low water soluble and/or low permeable [45]. Hydrophobic pharmaceuticals are delivered by several methods. For instance, a prodrug of an active agent can be developed to be metabolized to the desired medicine. Otherwise, particular functional groups are changed to produce synthetic analogues having greater hydrophilicity. Also, some compounds are formulated to allow oral delivery or other routes like intravenous or rectal administration. All of these methods have been fruitful to deliver hydrophobic therapeutics [46]. Nevertheless, such approaches may cause higher first pass effects and thereby the necessity to use a greater dosage, higher expense and even low patient compliance depending on the dosage form. Hence, novel drug delivery platforms must be designed and fabricated by both theoretical [47–49] and experimental [50] methods.

An effective path to deliver hydrophobic drugs is using polymer drug delivery platforms which are prepared as colloids, liposomes, micelles, dendrimers, carbon nanotubes and nanofibers that are commonly known as nanoparticle drug delivery vehicles [51]. The polymeric nanocarriers can favorably be concentrated in inflammation sites, tumors and at antigens through the enhanced permeability and retention, EPR, influence by vasculatures [42]. When the polymeric vehicles are accumulated at these sites, they act as drug depots to release drugs when necessary. This can give rise to sustained/controlled release, enhanced bioavailability and diminished toxicity.

In this review, the most recent achievements on the big family of micro- and nanoscale drug delivery systems (DDSs) will be presented. Such systems can be classified as inorganic nanomaterials (carbon nanomaterials, non-metals, metals, metal oxides and porous materials), polymeric nanocomposites (natural biopolymers, synthetic polymers/dendrimers and proteins, peptides, phospholipids, liposome and DNA) and smart systems (pH-, temperature-, glutathione (GSH)-, reactive oxygen species (ROS)-, hypoxia-, photo/light-, magnetic field-, enzyme-, electrochemical potential/redox-, glucose-, microwave-, ultrasound- and ion-responsive systems) as well as drug conjugates which can be classified as antibody-drug, polymer-drug, carbohydrate-drug, protein-drug, peptide-drug, lipid-drug and nanomaterial-drug conjugates, plus t-cell dual affinity re-targeting conjugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nano and microscale drug delivery systems (DDSs)
ce:section.ce:para: Application of controlled DDSs was begun in the early 1950s accompanied by developing transdermal and oral sustained release formulations which was continued with preparing zero order release carriers, micro- and nano-scales as well as self-regulated drug delivery platforms [52]. This field was quickly grown and resulted in the development of abundant innovative materials used in controlled therapeutics delivery such as microneedles, micro-reservoir implants, nanoparticles, transdermal patches and drug-antibody conjugates. Controlled DDSs could increase the administration, effectiveness and pharmacokinetics of pharmaceutics like vaccines, enzymes, peptides and others. Such systems increase therapeutics bioavailability through uptake enhancement, stopping premature degradation, preserving drug at the therapeutic windows and decreasing side effect through targeting drug into a specific tissue/cell [53]. A DDS with controlled release of drug with the anticipated amount would enable patient to receive on-demand, tunable and reproducible dosage on time [54]. Indeed, these systems lead to precise regulations of dosages for favorite effects, minimize side effects, prevent frequent drug administration or device implantation and eventually enhance the patient compliance.

Nanotechnology has shown a rapid development in therapy and diagnosis areas especially using pharmaceutical nanocarriers called nanomedicine. The nanoscale aggregates known as nanocarriers can be classified to nanoparticles (prepared from hydrogels, polymers, metals, ceramics), nanoburrs and lipid based nanocarriers (e.g., niosomes and liposomes) [55]. The successful targeted drug delivery achieved by nanomedicine has attracted great attention in order to develop nanobiological technologies to be applied for treatment, drug delivery and molecular imaging. Indeed, nanoparticles display some advantages as therapeutic and diagnostic agents in advanced drug delivery. Firstly, traditional drugs existing for injectable or oral administration are not often manufactured as the optimal formulations. The products incorporated with nucleic acids or proteins need other innovative types of carrier systems to increase their efficiency and to protect them from possible degradation [56]. The effectiveness of most drug vehicles has a direct dependence on their particle sizes (except for solution and intravenous). Because of their large surface area and small size, drug delivery nanoparticles exhibit enhanced solubility, improved bioavailability and increased capability to transport from the blood brain barrier, to pass the pulmonary tissues and to be absorbed by the tight joints of the endothelial skin cells [57]. It is noteworthy that nanoparticles created using both biodegradable and non-biodegradable synthetic and natural polymers are of high interest due to they are employed in targeted drug delivery with improved bioavailability to offer controlled medicine release using a single dosage because such systems can inhibit drug degradation by the endogenous enzymes. Secondly, designing innovative DDSs motivates the pharmaceutical companies to develop innovative more effective formulations that are beneficial to the patients.

It is known that nanocarriers are utilized to transport various chemotherapeutic drugs, proteins, nucleic acids, inhibitors and contrast agents which have revealed remarkable potential for the targeted pharmaceutics delivery [58]. The most commonly investigated complexes of drug-nanocarrier, nanomedicines, are polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, micelles, metallic nanoparticles (iron oxide or gold), nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes and quantum dots [59]. Some nanomedicines have so far been approved for clinical applications; for example, the first nanomedicine approved by the US Food and Drug Administration, FDA, was doxorubicin, DOX, encapsulated liposome injection called doxil1 used to treat ovarian cancer, multiple myeloma and AIDS related Kaposi's cancer [60]. Abraxane1 (albumin-bound paclitaxel) is also an FDA-approved nanomedicine that illustrates enhanced therapeutic index compared to paclitaxel alone to treat progressive non-small cell lung sarcoma and metastatic breast cancers [61]. Such nanomedicines exhibit improved physicochemical characteristics (stability and solubility) compared to conventional drugs. Furthermore, they can extend the drug circulation period by PEGylation, i.e. the polyethylene glycol, PEG, chains are attached nanocarriers to cause steric repulsions with the opsonins in blood [62].

Microcarriers are very important in the biomedicine field due to their widespread application as drug delivery systems [63]. The microcarriers illustrate more controlled drug level and improved pharmacokinetics through sustained and prolonged drug release compared to conventional drug delivery forms that require frequent long-term oral intake or injection [64]. Microcarriers have shown valuable properties such as compatibility with dynamic cell culture, high surface-to-volume ratio and mimicking natural extracellular environments [65]. The microcarriers are employed as platforms to deliver drugs, cells, proteins and as tissue engineering materials. They have versatile compositions and designs as ceramics, metals, polymers and their composites which have been prepared as solid-filled, porous and hollow microcarriers [64].

Nanomaterial-based drug delivery have become a hot topic in researches performed at the interface of nanotechnology and biomedicine due to it can bring about proficient loading, targeted delivery and controlled release of drugs [66]. Advances achieved in nanotechnology have led to considerable development of therapeutics in nanomedicine for different medicinal purposes such as therapy, targeting and imaging [67]. For instance, superparamagnetic quantum dots, iron oxide nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), dendrimers and micelles have indicated promising nano-medical applications as drug delivery vehicles, therapeutic and imaging agents [68]. Inorganic nanoparticles display numerous beneficial physical properties including high surface area (mesoporous materials), strong optical absorption, phosphorescence (doped oxides) and photoluminescence (quantum dots), magnetic moment (iron oxide) and metallic properties [69]. The surface functionalization has a strong effect on interactions of nanoparticles with their surrounding environments. In the following, application of some inorganic nanomaterials including carbon nanomaterials, Non-metals, metals and metal oxides and nanoporous materials as drug delivery systems will be presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanomaterials (graphene, carbon nanotubes, fullerene derivatives and carbon quantum dots)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is one of carbon allotropes (in addition to graphite, carbon nanotubes and fullerenes), which is composed of a flat single layer of carbon atoms in a honeycomb framework [70]. Graphene shows several exceptional characteristics such as a unique structure as well as thermal, mechanical and optical properties. Graphene-based nanosheets having lateral nanoscale sizes are graphene nanosheets (GNS), graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [71]. The GNS are broadly used as nanocarriers of various chemical and biological drugs. They are also exploited as effective nanocarriers for water-insoluble chemotherapy drugs. Beside small molecules, some biomolecules like peptides, proteins and nucleic acids are delivered by GO, rGO and graphene nanosheets. Though nucleic acids are macromolecules that are very hydrophile, ring structures of their nucleobases interact with the GO nanosheets through π-π stacking [72].

The physicochemical properties and facile uniform synthesis of graphene make it an auspicious platform for peptide loading. The peptide immobilization on GNS was attributed to the existence of cationic or aromatic amino acid residues on peptides [73]. For instance, the favored attachment of aromatic amino acids (tyrosine, tryptophan, histidine and phenylalanine) onto carbon nanotubes and graphene was occurred through π-π stacking contacts. It was exhibited that GO interaction was more effective with lysine, arginine and histidine than phenylalanine, tryptophan and tyrosine due to their stronger electrostatic attractions with the negative charges on the GO [74]. Similarly, proteins are adsorbed onto the GO through interactions with its functional groups [75]. It was established that GO-based materials as the protein carriers had an extraordinary capability of protein loading by physisorption than other vehicles. In order to have an efficient loading of proteins, the protein's surface must be modified to enhance its capacity to bind the GNS.

The graphene based nanomaterials indicating high near-infrared, NIR, absorption could be used as effective photothermal compounds in cancer theranostic applications for the cancer photothermal therapy [76]. Furthermore, several inorganic nanoparticles were anchored on the surface of the nano-graphene to induce extra optical and magnetic characteristics which are valuable to make contrast in multimodal tumor imaging in vivo [77]. The functionalized nano-graphene that is labeled by radionuclides like 66Ga, 64Cu, 131I and 125I can be employed in cancer radiotherapy and nuclear imaging [77]. Also, other graphene based nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons are applied as the drug nano-carriers.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanoparticles were synthesized to produce GQDs@imatinib via its reaction with carbodiimide crosslinker [79]. The cellular internalization of the GQDs@imatinb was in vitro assessed using several cancer cells such as leukemia plus adherent cancerous cells by the cellular apoptosis/viability tests. It was shown that imatinib attachment over the GQDs surface could not vary characteristics and shape of nanoparticles. Also, the GQDs@imatinib effectively internalized and killed cancerous cells through apoptosis stimulation confirming it could be efficiently used in treatment of cancers and principally leukemia.

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) was applied to functionalize GO via cystamine (Cys) linker containing disulfide bond and after that it was bound to polyethylene glycol (PEG) by host-guest interaction between β-CD and PEG end-capped with adamantane [80]. The DOX anticancer medicine was loaded into the GO-Cys-CD-PEG and a large loading capacity (94.58%) was measured. In-vitro DOX release test proved that the DOX was not released in physiological medium and great DOX release was happened in acidic cancerous cells environment as well as upon the GSH simulation by intracellular redox environs. These phenomena exhibited that the GO-Cys-CD-PEG was a favorable dual responsive drug carrier. Besides, it displayed a high stability in physiological salt, substantial cyto-compatibility plus considerably greater cytotoxicity compared to pure DOX against HepG2 cells. These results were associated with stabilization by PEG and boosted GO-Cys-CD-PEG/DOX dispersion within cell culture milieu which is required in effective drug transport.

Dacarbazine as an anticancer drug was adsorbed on outer and inner surfaces of bare plus carboxylic acid functionalized CNTs in water and the interactions were studied via molecular dynamics simulations [87]. It was shown that the dacarbazine was spontaneously encapsulated in the cavities of all bare as well as the functionalized CNTs. Particularly, π-π interactions controlled the dacarbazine configuration within the bare as well as the functionalized CNTs.

Recently, a magnetic nanocomposite hydrogel of poly(acrylicacid) and Fe3O4 NPs@MWCNT was prepared to decrease the fast clearance of the Fe3O4 NPs@MWCNT via the blood stream prior to their recognition by the target tissues [88]. Moreover, swelling, load and release kinetics of tetracycline hydrochloride drug were examined within several solutions.

Fullerenes with nanometric sizes can be used in pharmacological and therapeutic applications. These DDSs exhibit superior benefits over traditional large-scale systems such as improved pharmacokinetic behaviors and enhanced payload [89]. Indeed, they are suitable drug carriers because they indicate proficient drug loading and release, formulation stability, high shelf life, biocompatibility, biodistribution, targeting and functionalization capability. Relative simplicity of their surface chemistry modification allows binding of therapeutic and targeting agents for specific therapeutic purposes. Among various fullerenes, C60 is a nanoparticle containing 32 aromatic rings [90]. The postulated non-toxicity, nanosize, antioxidant potency, capacity to interact with biomolecules and ability to enter cell membranes for the C60 have led to its applications in medicine. Also, it may increase the efficacy of common antineoplastic agents and simultaneously reduce their adverse effects. The C60 fullerene was shown to be an anticancer prodrug [91]. The C60 fullerenes can serve as drug carriers, anti-HIV protease inhibitors and promising medications for allergies and other inflammatory diseases.

As fullerenes have revealed desirable capacities for drug transport as well as photodynamic treatment, DOX-incorporated poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI) modified C60 fullerene named C60–PEI–DOX was achieved and used in combined photodynamic- and chemo-therapy [92]. Also, covalent conjugate of DOX with C60–PEI was synthesized using pH-responsive hydrazone bond. The DOX release from C60–PEI–DOX was highly pH-dependent. The C60–PEI–DOX biodistribution was explored via injection of conjugate labeled with CdSe/ZnS QDs, called C60–PEI–DOX/QDs, to mice bearing tumors. C60–PEI–DOX/QDs exhibited greater antitumor efficacy that pure QDs. Also, the C60–PEI–DOX illustrated superior in vivo antitumor capacity than bare DOX against murine tumor, without any noticeable toxicity influences onto normal tissues because of its faborable tumor targeting effect and 2.4 times larger DOX release quantity into tumor compared to the normal organs. As well, C60–PEI–DOX revealed substantial synergistic antitumor potential throughout photodynamic treatment. Thus, C60–PEI–DOX was introduced and suggested for cancer therapy as it showed high effectiveness and minimum side effects.

In addition to the three-dimensional graphite, two-dimensional graphene and graphene oxide and one-dimensional CNTs, carbon dots (C-dots) or carbon quantum dots (QDs), that are zero-dimensional carbon based nanomaterials, can be considered as very attractive nontoxic substitutes of semiconductor QDs [93,94]. The C-dots are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm. They are inherently less toxic than other semiconductor-based QDs so that the in vitro cytotoxicity tests on C-dots have shown that they are non-toxic [95]. Moreover, the in vivo cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of C-dots examined on mice [96] and zebrafish [97] proved that they had no obvious toxicity. Because C-dots have exceptional photoluminescence properties, versatile and facile synthetic routes, great biocompatibility and tunable surface functionalities they have wide applications as drug delivery systems, sensors, bioimaging agents, catalysts, optoelectronic and energy storage/conversion materials.

It is known that delivery of therapeutics using highly biocompatible and water soluble C-dots is an effective approach in order to adjust the drug release rate in biological media. In this context, dopamine hydrochloride, DA, as the most imperative inotropic vasopressor compound consumed for neurological patients, was anchored to the C-dots to achieve in vitro controlled release carrier in physiological milieu [98]. The DA release time from the C-dots–DA at pH 7.4 was highly prolonged to 60 h relative to the control DA alone. Also, it was found that the C-dots–DA conjugate was biocompatible to Neuro 2A cell line with 74% survival rate using the highest concentration of 9 μg mL−1. The in vivo cytotoxicity test on thin tissues of mice revealed that conjugate did not impose any hostile effects or anatomical distortions on tissues. Moreover, body weights of mice injected with 20 μg mL−1 nano-conjugate through the tail vein exhibited negligible changes after 45 days. Fig. 8 indicates the internalization of the Neuro 2A cells in which Fig. 8a and c displays the bright field and blue fluorescent images of the cells incubated by the C-dotas as the control. Fig. 8b and d shows the blue fluorescent due to the C-dots–DA internalization into the cells. The number and shape of cells have not been changed confirming the nanomaterial biocompatibility. Fig. 9 demonstrates the heart, brain, spleen, liver and kidney tissues stained with Eosin and Haemotoxolin and treated using the DA and C-dots–DA.

Chemotherapy is commonly applied against several types of cancers. The chemotherapy drugs such as DOX, 5-fluorouracil, paclitaxel, vinblastine and methotrexate are sometimes combined to enhance their effectiveness. However, chemotherapeutic drugs are not selective to cancer cells as they also can kill healthy normal cells which cause severe side effects [99]. The damaging and harmful chemotherapeutics distribution is considered as one of the most significant difficulties in chemotherapy. Currently, C-dots are employed in cancer therapy due to their low toxicity and biocompatibility. The C-dots/DOX antitumor drug complexes were prepared to be used for cancer treatment [100]. The complexes of C-dots/DOX displayed that the DOX release was pH-dependent and the cytotoxicity test verified that they were not cytotoxic. Also, in comparison to the free DOX drug, the complexes of C-dots/DOX indicated considerably more destruction of the ACC-2 adenoid cystic cancer cells whereas less toxicity to the L929 mouse fibroblast cells. Flow cytometry and confocal microscopy images established that C-dots/DOX complexes improved cancer therapy efficacy by internalizing much higher amount of drug in the tumor cells nuclei and caused more apoptosis to the ACC-2 cells relative to the free DOX.

The PEG/C-dots was designed and prepared as pseudo-spherical nanoparticles of ~12 nm diameters indicating fluorescence property, satisfactory stability, appropriate water solubility and a 28.58% remarkable quantum yield [101]. The PEG/C-dots were loaded by DOX anticancer drug at pH 9.0 indicating 24.25 mg/g drug loading capacity and 77.23% encapsulation efficiency. It was shown that the DOX release after 23 h within the phosphate buffer saline, PBS, solution at pH 5.5 reached 76.67%.

Quinic acid, as an antioxidant with anticancer capacity via apoptosis assisted toxicity to breast cancerous cells, was conjugated with nitrogen-doped CQD nanoparticles to target breast cancer tissue [102]. Notably, quinic acid has a high affinity to selectins that are angiogenesis agents augmented within breast cancerous cells. Gemcitabine medicine was also incorporated onto these nanoparticles by electrostatic attractions. Cellular proliferation was assessed using MCF7 cells. In vivo biodistribution and imaging tests were done by growth of mice breast cancer tissue. It was found that the quinic acid/N-CQDs conjugate had auspicious features like outstanding luminescent and significant accumulation in tumor which suggested it was an exceptional multifunctional candidate for chemotherapy and theranostic agent.

Silica nanoparticles are promising non-metal DDSs exhibiting their valuable advantages among other inorganic and organic nanoparticles [103]. Certainly, they exhibit biocompatibility, which have been identified as safe materials by the U.S. FDA, GRAS numbers 554 and 321 [104]. They are produced in various shapes and sizes in large scales; also, they are simply functionalized and used for the encapsulation of contrast/active materials for application in both diagnostic and therapeutic areas. As well, orthosilicic acid is their main biodegradation product that is quickly absorbed and then excreted through renal clearance organ.

It is well recognized that drug targeting by means of functionalized NPs is an efficient standard strategy to guide drug transport into desired sites in cancer treatments such as anticancer photodynamic therapy [105]. Recently, non-porous and porous silica-based nanoparticles having dissimilar chemical modifications were used as DDSs for the photosensitizer temoporfin [106]. The efficacy of nanoparticles was higher compared to that of commercial temoporfin for phototoxicity in vitro on 4T1 mammary murine carcinoma and anticancer influence in vivo on MDA-MB-231 tumor bearing Nu/Nu mice. The temoporfin loaded silica NPs passed through the blood brain barrier confirming their capability to treat brain metastases.

Hybrid organic-inorganic nanomaterials were synthesized based on dextran aldehyde (Dex-CHO) and dextran (Dex) polymers used to coat inorganic silica aerogels which were then loaded with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) drug and applied as enzyme sensitive for colon cancer therapy [107]. The silica aerogel surface was modified by 3-(aminopropyl)triethoxysilane which enhanced drug loading capacities of Dex-CHO and Dex. Dextranase enzyme was used to perform enzyme sensitive drug release tests within simulated colon fluid. Also, aerogels toxicity onto the colorectal adenocarcinoma (Caco-2) cells was explored. The 5-FU release from Dex-CHO and Dex coated silica aerogels within simulated intestinal and gastric solutions were 3.4 and 1.7%, respectively but the 5-FU release from non-coated silica aerogel was 86.4% within such solutions. However, 5-FU release amounts after 12 h within the colonic milieu comprising dextranase, upon dextran degradation with dextranase, were 13.4 and 24%, respectively, from Dex-CHO and Dex coated silica aerogels. The bare, amine-functionalized, Dex-CHO and Dex coated silica aerogels had not any substantial cytotoxicity effects onto the Caco-2 cells but 5-FU incorporated bare, amine-functionalized, Dex-CHO and Dex coated silica aerogels decreased the Caco-2 cells proliferation. Hence, Dex-CHO and Dex coated silica aerogel nanoparticles exhibited biocompatibility which was not altered through upper gastrointestinal areas confirming they were influential enzyme responsive drug carriers for colon cancer treatment.

Advances achieved in nanotechnology have led to therapeutic developments in nanomedicine which deals with several medical applications such as imaging, targeting and therapy. Recently, superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, quantum dots, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been used as potential drug carriers, imaging and therapeutic materials. AuNPs are specifically applied in various fields. AuNPs reveal high surface area to volume ratios and hence lead to the drug/biomolecule attachment onto their surfaces [108]. Moreover, AuNPs have been synthesized via several techniques in diverse shapes and sizes.

The oligonucleotides incorporated with DOX were bound onto AuNPs and employed as DDSs for cancer chemotherapy (Fig. 10 ) [109]. It was found that 80% DOX was linked onto the oligonucleotides over the AuNPs and created DOX-oligomer-AuNPs (DOA) so that ~28% DOX release was measured. The antitumor activity in vivo and cytotoxicity in vitro of the drug were confirmed using colon cancer SW480 cells and xenograft mouse. The viability of the SW480 cells incubated for 24 h by the DOA was 41.77%. Also, the DOA was repeatedly administered to tumor-bearing mouse and the antitumor activity inspected as the tumor size was measured indicating the ratio of treatment to control was 0.69 which verified the DOA was a favorable carrier vehicle in treatment of the colorectal tumor.

In another research, synthesis of amphiphilic chitosan grafted cetylalcohol-maleic anhydride-pyrazinamide, CS-g-(CA-MA-PZA), was performed that was loaded with rifampicin and entrapped with AgNPs [111]. Fig. 13 demonstrates the cell apoptosis of Ag NPs and rifampicin-loaded CS-g-(CA-MA-PZA) micelles against THP-1 cells after 12, 24 and 48 h but Fig. 14 presents their cellular uptake after 6, 12, 24 and 48 h. The cell uptake, cell apoptosis and cell viability in vitro tests exhibited that the multi-drug carrier improved the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility effects on the cells. Hence, it was found that this system was a promising candidate to immediately treat the alveolar macrophages.

It is known that contact lenses are extensively employed in controllable ophthalmic drug release as they can solve the problems of eye drops [112]. However, drug containing contact lenses provided through common soaking procedure display low uptake of medicine, great burst release, and changes in critical characteristics of lenses. Therefore, influences of AuNPs onto ketotifen uptake and drug release rate from lens were examined. For this purpose, AuNPs solutions with different concentrations were added to ketotifen solution (mM-AuNPs-SS batches). Also, in second process, AuNPs were loaded into contact lenses throughout production (mM-AuNPs-L batches). The AuNPs showed a spherical shape, 17.2 nm mean diameter and −21.2 mV zeta potential. The light transmittance, oxygen permeability and swelling of contact lenses were enhanced relative to ones fabricated using common soaking technique (K-SM-L). The mM-AuNPs-L lenses exhibited significantly boosted ketotifen drug uptake than mM-AuNPs-SS and K-SM-L lenses. The in vitro tests proved that less burst release but controlled ketotifen release was happened within 96 h, in comparison to 48 h for K-SM-L lenses. The sustained ketotifen release was increased dependent on increasing the AuNPs within the lenses. The AuNPs decreased adherence of proteins to the contact lenses. Besides, the AuNPs incorporated lenses were safe based on ocular irritation and histopathology tests performed on rabbits. Additionally, they exhibited extended ketotifen retaining within the rabbit tear fluid than eye drops and K-SM-L lenses. Accordingly, AuNPs added to contact lenses intensified ketotifen sorption from soaking solution, declined burst release, boosted in vivo and in vitro release rates and did not affect critical lens characteristics which was crucial for therapeutic purposes.

Fe3O4 nanostructures indicating mean diameters of 9.5, 4.3 and 2.1 nm were synthesized and their biological and photothermal properties were examined as medication carriers into MCF-7 cells [113]. Fe3O4 nanostructures with diameter <5 nm entered nuclei whereas bigger particles (diameter~9.5 nm) just situated within the cytoplasm. Fe3O4 nanostructures all exhibited desirable photothermal capacity, triggered the DOX release incorporated onto the nanomaterials and killed cancerous cells. Fe3O4 nanostructures with 4.3 and 2.1 nm diameters demonstrated greater cytotoxicity than 9.5 nm nanomaterials because they had exceptional nuclei targeting potential. Therefore, designing drug carriers by this process enhanced the therapeutic effectiveness.

Zinc oxide nanoparticles were doped with egg albumin and called ZnO-Ea NPs which displayed an ultraviolet (UV) absorption peak at 310 nm [114]. The ZnO-Ea NPs had a hexagonal platelet-like and spherical shape with mean diameters of 20–60 nm. MCF-7 cancer cells were used to estimate the biocompatibility and anticancer capacities of ZnO-Ea NPs. The ZnO-Ea NPs exhibited anticancer potency against MCF-7 confirming they had a substantial toxicity as they diminished proliferation of cells. Western blot and gene expression studies illustrated that the ZnO-Ea NPs induced ROS generation which controlled mRNA transcription of apoptotic genes including caspase-9, caspase-3, bcl-2 and p53 but considerably decreased expressing Bcl-2 anti-apoptotic gene. Accordingly, ZnO-Ea NPs particularly repressed the MCF-7 gene expression via ROS production plus toxicity mediated cell decease confirming it was suitable for cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Porous materials (zeolites, MOFs, MCMs and SBAs)
ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical sciences employ porous nanomaterials to diminish the drug side effects and toxicity [115,116]. For this purpose, numerous DDSs with various structures such as microporous zeolites, mesoporous MCM and SBA silicates as well as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are used as potential drug delivery platforms due to their low toxicity, controlled drug release and high payloads [117–120].

Zeolites are crystalline inorganic aluminosilicates known as molecular sieve adsorbents that constitute uniform microporous three-dimensional architectures with large internal surface areas that can be close to or greater than 1000 m2/g [121]. They are employed as appropriate pharmaceutical carrier systems to increase the dissolution of drugs having inherent low aqueous solubility and efficiently control drug release in a reproducible and expected manner. Using hierarchical zeolites and mesoporous materials is a useful method to modulate the drug release rate. In this context, zeolites were utilized as carriers to deliver both lipophilic and hydrophilic drugs, anti-neoplastic compounds as well as DNA and nucleic acids [122].

The anticancer 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) drug was incorporated into Faujasite zeolites (NaY, 700 nm and nanoNaY, 150 nm) having diverse particle sizes and Linde type L as potassium form, LTL, indicating 80 nm diameter [123]. The drug release in vitro tests in PBS with pH 7.4 at 37 °C indicated that 80–90% 5-FU was released in 10 min. Also, first-order, zero-order, Hixson–Crowell, Higuchi, Weibull and Korsmeyer–Peppas kinetic models were used to fit the release data in order to determine release kinetics. The in vitro release by the zeolite DDSs was fitted to the Weibull model. Besides, the efficacies of such DDSs were assessed by means of both human colorectal cancer cells including RKO and HCT-15. It was found that the unloaded zeolites did not have any cytotoxicity to these carcinoma cells but the drug containing DDSs significantly affected the cells viability and the immunofluorescence analysis afforded confirmation for the cell internalization by the zeolites.

In another research, superparamagnetic magnetite nanoparticles were synthesized on/in zeolite to achieve magnetite/zeolite nanocomposites, MZNC, and loaded with 5-FU [124]. The drug-loaded nanoparticles displayed a sustained drug release without any burst release. The cytotoxic effects of 5-FU loaded nanoparticles were examined on human gastric carcinoma cells and it was shown that cells lost their cell membranes integrity upon apoptosis confirming the 5-FU loaded nanoparticles were beneficial for the cancer treatment. The morphological changes and apoptosis of AGS cells treated using various amounts of 5-FU-MZNC were investigated. Considering morphological variations, it was found that after 24 h treatment by 25, 50, and 100 mg/mL of 5-FU-MZNC, the cell proliferation was considerably decreased and the number of apoptotic cells was substantially enhanced was in comparison to the untreated cells.

Zeolites NaY faujasite and LTL (Linde Type L) were used as DDSs to load the 5-FU pharmaceutical and their in vitro anticancer activities were assessed against melanoma, colorectal carcinoma and breast cancer cells as well as in vivo by means of chick embryo chorioallantoic tissue [125]. The most favorable results were achieved using 5-FU encapsulated LTL zeolite against higher potentiation Hs578T breast cancerous cells. The endocytic mechanism of zeolite nanomaterials intracellular localization was explored through pharmacologically inhibition of endocytosis within human epithelial mammary and breast cancer cells. It was suggested that caveolin facilitated procedure induced zeolite nanomaterials internalization. The effectiveness of the LTL zeolite DDS with as disc form was enhanced via functionalizing its external surface by COOH or NH2 groups that was then modified by poly-l-lysine. The modified LTL nanomaterial with a positively charged surface was not toxic to human cells and essentially it was quicker internalized and exhibited greater in vivo tumor decrease verifying it was a very appropriate anticancer drug carrier.

Zeolite Y nanostructure was developed as pH responsive DDS for the cisplatin (CIS) drug loaded into the cavities of zeolite Y and loading capacities of 32.4 ± 0.3 to 19.3 ± 0.6% were measured that were dependent on CIS concentration, time and pH [126]. Besides, Y/CIS NPs illustrated a pH sensitive drug release proved via in vitro CIS release tests. Remarkably, at acidic pH = 5.4, quicker CIS release was obtained, i.e. 93.8 ± 6.8% versus 50.6 ± 2.4% in seven days incubation. Though the Y/CIS NPs displayed cytotoxicity upon releasing the CIS, the zeolite nanostructure was not cytotoxic and enhanced MG63 cells proliferation relative to the pure CIS. The Y/CIS was highly cytocompatible, physiologically stable and indicated controlled pH dependent CIS release confirming it could be suitable as the nanocarrier of chemotherapeutic medications.

MOFs are organic–inorganic hybrid materials formed as metal clusters interconnected by organic linkers [15]. They display several favorite features as drug carriers such as extraordinarily high surface areas and large pore sizes that are suitable for drug encapsulation, large loadings, controlled drug release, inherent biodegradability and various functionalities for post-synthetic grafting of drugs [127]. Compared to other drug carriers, the MOFs DDSs exhibit stronger hydrogen bond, coordination bond and anions-cations electrostatic interactions with drugs that are beneficial to attain stable drug release rates.

Recently, Cu3(BTC)2, BTC = 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate, MOF was employed to load guaiacol, anethole and ibuprofen with loading amounts of 0.40, 0.38 and 0.34 g g−1, respectively, which showed controlled drug release [128]. In another work, mesoporous MIL-101(Fe)–NH2 MOF was prepared and functionalized using bis(3-aminopropylamino)ethane (bap) and ethylenediamine (en) 1,2- having diverse chain lengths (Fig. 15 ) [129]. The three MIL-101(Fe)–NH2, MIL-101(Fe)–NH2-en and MIL-101(Fe)–NH2-bap were used as drug vehicles to deliver non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug naproxen. The naproxen release was carried out in simulated intravenous solution, pH 7.4, and simulated gastric fluid, pH 2, and it was found that the naproxen release was dependent to both of the pH value and amine functional groups so that the amine groups amounts in bap and en more significantly affected release rates than the pH value.

The linear 1,4-benzenedi(4′-pyrazolyl) ligand was reacted with Zn(II) and Ni(II) cations to synthesize stable MOFs which revealed high porosity and exceptional stability in pH range of 2–13 [130]. The two MOFs were applied as drug carriers and diclofenac sodium load/release experiments showed that Ni(II) and Zn(II) MOFs had the greatest loading capacities of 89.4 and 209.8 mg/g in 12 h, respectively. The equilibrium concentration of released drug in PBS during 48 h were 74.9 and 89.8% DCF using the Ni(II) and Zn(II) MOFs showing a slower drug release rate from the Ni(II) MOF [130]. The [Zn2(L)(H2O)1.5].5H2O (1) was prepared as a drug carrier for 5-FU anticancer drug with the 5-FU drug incorporation into the 1 was ~48.6 wt% per gram of dehydrated 1 [131]. A very controlled drug release was measured with 98% of the drug was released in 101 h at acidic medium and 62% in 72 h in PBS. Also, the in vitro cytotoxicity test proved that the MOF 1 did not display any significant cytotoxicity. Fig. 16 shows the MTT cytotoxicity tests performed using HEK293 and HELA cells treated by different concentrations of H4L, Zn(NO3)2, 1 and 1′@5-FU.

ZIF-8 (zeolite imidazolate framework-8) MOF was used to load indomethacin (IDM) known as the antipyretic and analgesic drug with low water solubility [132]. The IDM amount adsorbed onto the ZIF-8 was 1.21 g/g ZIF-8. X-ray diffraction data displayed that the crystal phase of IDM deposited onto the ZIF-8 looked like its η-form. Also, IDM water solubility was 45 folds enhanced upon its loading onto the ZIF-8 compared to the pure γ-form IDM. Thus, the ZIF-8 MOF could extremely improve pharmaceutics water solubility.

A phenolic coumarin compound called scopoletin (SC) is usually obtained from several medical plants and demonstrates hypoglycemic, hypocholesterolemic, antitumor, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory capacities [133]. Loading of SC in situ was done using ZIF-8 as the sustained release DDS and 22.5% encapsulation efficiency was achieved. The SC release quantities into the medium at pH amounts of 6.5 and 4.5 were measured using high performance liquid chromatography. The SC release from the ZIF-8 was pH sensitive relatives to the bare SC. All release data obtained were well matched Peppas-Sahlin and Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetics at pH values of 6.5 and 4.5, respectively. Furthermore, SC exhibited expectorant activity using 100 and 30 mg/kg dosages whereas it expectorant activity was not seen using 10 mg/kg concentration.

Ordered mesoporous silica materials such as MCM and SBA compounds can be used as platforms for different drugs due to their outstanding loading capacity and capacity to control the drug uptake and release by modulating the interactions between loaded molecules and support [134]. Several approaches have been carried out to tailor the properties of silica matrixes such as controlling the geometry and pore size, surface functionalization by diverse organic groups and incorporation of heteroatoms in the silica frameworks.

In another effort, cystamine functionalized periodic mesoporous organosilica, named Cys-PMO, was prepared as redox and pH dual sensitive nanoparticle and loaded by the DOX anticancer medicine to be used in intracellular cancer therapy [135]. The Cys-PMO nanomaterial exhibited 691 m2/g surface area, 0.59 cm3/g pore volume and 3.1 nm pore size. Also, it revealed 50.6% DOX loading (15.2 mg DOX/30 mg Cys-PMO) using optimal DOX concentration (20 mg) and prevented early drug release in extracellular environments. It was found that the in vitro the DOX loaded Cys-PMO had considerably superior cytotoxicity to HeLa cells within redox and intracellular acidic pH environments which was associated with degrading the disulfide bonds existing in the Cys-PMO structure. Additionally, confocal microscopy image showed that the DOX containing Cys-PMO material was localized into the HeLa cells. After Cys-PMO was internalized in HeLa cells, it released the DOX molecules into the nucleus through the intracellular redox and pH triggers. Accordingly, the redox and pH responsive Cys-PMO nanohybrid was an appropriate candidate for intracellular pharmaceutics release.

Mesoporous silica nanoparticles or MSNs were coated with chitosan oligosaccharide through ligand–metal coordination complex bonds and the fabricated MSNs@COS nanomaterial was employed as pH sensitive drug carrier [136]. The MSNs@COS NPs showed large DOX loading capacity, suitable drug maintenance efficacy at physiological pH = 7.4 plus intracellular pH sensitive drug release activity in acidic pH values of 4.0, 5.0 and 6.5. Besides, the intracellular uptake and the biocompatibility of the MSNs@COS were estimated using MDA-MB-231 cells. The microscopic fluorescence image and in vitro cytotoxicity tests verified that the MSNs@COS was biocompatible and easily internalized the MDAMB-231 cells. Hence, the MSNs@COS was a favorable pH stimuli sensitive nanomaterial indicating controlled release of anticancer drug.

Mesoporous materials with superparamagnetic characteristics were synthesized through two diverse methods including direct incorporation and wet impregnation [137]. Then, the synthetized materials were employed as drug platforms that could load indomethacin drug. The release kinetics were examined by the Pepas and Sahlin model. It was found that materials modified by direct incorporation had a superior adsorption capability and slower release rate (Fig. 17 ) which was possibly associated with the existence of more silanol moieties over solid surfaces to interact with the indomethacin drug. In another study, ordered nanoporous silica MCM-48, VP = 0.63(2) cm3/g and SBET = 923(3) m2/g, was employed to deliver poorly soluble anti-inflammatory indomethacin drug [138]. The drug release from the pure and 3-aminopropyl functionalized MCM-48 carriers were performed in simulated body fluids having pH values of 7.4 and 2. The indomethacin contents were 21 and 45 wt% in MCM-48 and amine functionalized MCM-48, respectively. Compared to the pure MCM-48, slower drug release rates were measured for the functionalized MCM-48 carriers containing various drug concentrations at the two pH values. The maximum released drug was 97% at pH 7.4 from unmodified silica and the least quantity was obtained (57%) at pH 2 for the amino functionalized MCM-48. Also, it was indicated that release kinetics obeyed the Higuchi model.

Hollow mesoporous silica (HMS) Mobil and Composition of Matter No.41 (MCM-41) were employed to encapsulate bicalutamide (BLT) anticancer medicine and large BLT loading values of 62 and 40% were measured for the HMS and MCM-41, respectively [139]. Also, application of HMS and MCM-41 materials as DDSs decreased the early burst BLT release and a slower BLT release was observed than the bare BLT. The pure and BLT incorporated HMS and MCM-41 nanoparticles were blood biocompatible. According to cytotoxicity assays, the BLT loaded HMS and MCM-41 materials revealed higher in vitro antitumor potentials towards LNCaP prostate adenocarcinoma cells in the order of HMS/BLT > MCM-41/BLT > BLT. Hence, these BLT drug carriers provided sustained medicine bioavailability, suitable non-toxicity and improved in vitro antitumor capacity to LNCaP cells.

Recently, bifunctional amine functionalized mesoporous SBA (Santa Barbara Amorphous type) silica, SBA-NH2, was applied as a DDS for cefazolin drug indicating extended drug release as well as mineralization feature [140]. The SBA-NH2 revealed a higher adsorption efficacy for the cefazolin and a prolonged drug release of seven days in comparison to the pure SBA-15. Furthermore, the SBA-NH2 displayed mineralization ability once it was immersed into the simulated body fluid that exhibited postponed generation of hydroxycarbonate apatite relative to the pure SBA-15 that did not disturb controlled release of cefazolin. After 28 days, a hydroxycarbonate apatite layer mimicking the bone mineral was created over the surface of SBA-NH2 in simulated body fluid. Nanorods of SBA-15 indicting large surface area of 1010 m2g−1 were modified using three diverse alkoxysilanes [141]. Then, the nanocarriers were applied to transport the anticancer gemcitabine drug. The functionalized carriers revealed greater interactions with the drug which resulted in enhanced loading of the drug molecules. Moreover, the gemcitabine loading onto the modified mesoporous matrixes was depended on the amount and the type of alkoxysilane. The greatest drug amount loaded onto the functionalized SBA-15 samples was ~22 wt%. A pH dependent drug release was measured for the functionalized SBA-15 nanorods bearing NH2 moieties over the surface.

SBA-15 as a mesoporous family member of MSMs was utilized as the carrier of riluzole (RLZ) pharmaceutical which enhanced its water dissolution and produced stable amorphous RLZ within solution [142]. The ratio of SBA-15 to RLZ was 1:1 wt/wt.% and majority of RLZ molecules were trapped into the SBA-15 uniform mesoporous network which prevented homogeneously dispersed RLZ recrystallization in the mesopores. The RLZ release amount after 8 h from SBA-15 was nearly 98.51 ± 2.01% but pure RLZ exhibited merely 39.57% water solubility confirming the SBA-15 considerably increased drug solubility and it could be used to enhance the oral bioavailability of RLZ.

So far, numerous types of polymeric carriers and their nanocomposites have been used for the drug deliver such as natural and synthetic linear polymers, dendritic polymers (dendrimers and dendrons), liposomes, micelles, proteins, peptides, phospholipids, liposome and DNA [143]. The synthetic polymers (mainly the biodegradable polymers) and nanobiopolymers are very important as DDSs in biomedical and pharmaceutical applications. Natural and synthetic macromolecules are commonly applied as vehicles to deliver drugs, particularly hydrophobic drugs, because such vectors can enhance the drug solubility and avoid the burst release and adverse interactions to the biological materials [144,145]. Other delivery platforms including liposomes, micelles, proteins, peptides, phospholipids, liposome and DNA are also highly beneficial in achieving efficient drug delivery.

Natural polymers can be classified to protein-based polymers (albumin, collagen, gelatin) and polysaccharides (chitosan, cyclodextrin, alginate, starch, agarose, dextran, hyaluronic acid, cellulose) [5,23,30,33]. Both biodegradable and non-biodegradable synthetic polymers exist. The synthetic biodegradable polymers include (a) polyesters (polylactic acid, polyglycolic acid, polyhydroxyl butyrate, polycaprolactone, polyester, polylactide-co-glycolide (PLGA), poly diaxonone), (b) polyanhydride (polyadepicacid, polysebacicacid, polyterpthalicacid), (c) polyamides (polyaminoacid, polyiminocarbonate), (d) phosphorous based polymers (polyphosphates, polyphosphonates, polyphosphazenes) and others (polycyanoacrylates, polyurethanes, polyortho ester, polyacetals). The non-biodegradable polymers are (a) cellulose derivative (ethylcellulose, carboxymethylcellulose, celluloseacetate hydroxylpropyl methylcellulose), (b) silicons (polydimethylsiloxane, polymethacrylate, colloidal silica, polymethylmethacrylate) and (c) others (poloxamine, ethylvinylacetate, polyvinylpyrolidine).

Natural biodegradable polymers are enormously attractive materials for application in the controlled drug delivery because of their valuable characteristics like small size and simple preparation methods. The foremost benefit of drug containing biodegradable polymers is improved therapeutic drug influence, following lower dose necessity and decreased toxic side effects [25]. Chitosan (CS) and poly-D,l-lactide-co-glycolide, PLGA, are the two extensively utilized biodegradable, biocompatible and non-toxic biopolymers. PLGA is degraded to glycolic and lactic acids in the body which are used in natural metabolism of kreb's cycle and lastly eliminated as CO2 and H2O [16,30,35,37,38]. Likewise, CS is a biopolymer derived from chitin which is an excellent flocculent, biodegradable, biocompatible and non-toxic, antibacterial, antifungal and has the ability to adhere the negatively charged surfaces. It has an exceptional chemical structure that can act as a linear polyelectrolyte having a great charge density plus amino and hydroxyl reactive groups [11]. CS and its family are commonly employed as drug carriers [38]. The PLGA-modified CS NPs were used in effective drug delivery [12,146]; also CS was utilized as solid lipid NPs in efficient paclitaxel transport. The biocompatible polyethylene glycol (PEG) is of great interest for drug delivery [25]. It is a non-biodegradable polymer which displays features like biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, immunogenicity, protein resistance, suitable solubility in water and other solvents [16]. Additionally, it is found that PEGs could maintain their biological characteristics by reversing the immunogenicity of proteins [147]. PEGs are able to conjugate with proteins, peptides and non-peptide drugs [148]. Such conjugates are generally stable against enzymatic degradation, less antigenic and immunogenic [12]. As well, PEGs can be co-polymerized by aliphatic linear polyesters like poly(lactic acid) for application in DDSs, tissue engineering and to enhance the biocompatibility of polymers [15,16,25].

It is known that hollow biodegradable polymeric nanoparticles have effectively low density and high surface areas as drug carriers. Thus, the hollow CS nanospheres were obtained by means of the PLGA as the template and it was found that the particle size was increased from 125 to 186 nm upon creation of core@shell materials (Fig. 18 ) [149]. Also, the specific surface area was enhanced from 62 m2/g in CS@PLGA to 111 m2/g for the hollow CS nanospheres. The ramipril drug in acetone (1.5, 3 and 5 mg/mL) was loaded to the hollow CS nanospheres so that 91% entrapment efficiency and 96% drug content were measured for the carrier containing 5 mg/mL drug. The ramipril release amounts in vitro were 73 and 86% in phosphate (pH 6.3) and acetate (pH 3.3) buffers, respectively, but 48% ramipril was released in Tris buffer, pH 8.0. The Korsemeyer-Peppas kinetics model for the release proved that the ramipril release was controlled by the swelling.

In another study, pH-sensitive biopolymer hydrogels were achieved based on CS and PEG of diverse molecular weights with silane crosslinker [150]. The hydrogels components were not dissolved in various swelling solutions due to they were linked through siloxane linkages. The water swelling was increased upon adding PEG with a larger molecular weight. The hydrogels exhibited higher swellings in basic and acidic pH values while little swellings were observed in pH 7 and 6. This pH-responsive characteristic made the hydrogels appropriate for controlled injectable drug carriage. The cefixime controlled release from the CS-PEG hydrogels displayed that drug release was completely happened in simulated gastric fluid during 30 min but controlled drug release was occurred in 80 min (85% release) in simulated intestinal fluid. Hence, the developed carriers were suitable as injectable materials in biological pH.

Some pH-responsive nano-hydrogels were prepared using biopolymer tragacanth gum, TG, as drug transport platforms by means of diverse chemical reagents including 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane modifier and polyvinylalcohol (PVA), glyceroldiglycidylether (GDE) and glutaraldehyde (GA) crosslinkers [151]. The gel content was enhanced by increasing the crosslinker content and reached the highest value, 90%. Fig. 19 indicates the SEM images of TG, TG-GA5, TG-GA-PVA2 and TGAP-GDE2 hydrogels. The nanohydrogels swelling tests were carried out by changing the temperature (27, 37 and 60 °C), pH (9, 7.4 and 2.2) as well as the reaction time (2–24 h). The indomethacin loading onto the hydrogels and the drug release rates in vitro were dependent on the nanohydrogels structures. It was shown that 50–80% of drug was released at pH 9 in 24 h.

NIR photothermal treatment is commonly employed in biomedicine, antibacterial and anticancer applications because NIR can deeply penetrate into biological systems with no detrimental side effects [152]. Hence, NIR radiation was utilized to stimulate drug release from CS based hydrogels composed of covellite CuS nanospheres conjugated onto dopamine. CuS acted as the excellent NIR sensitive material and photothermal triggering agent which facilitated the medication release. The CS/dopamine@CuS composite was placed within dialysis bag and drug release upon NIR radiation was estimated. Cell proliferation or cytotoxicity and flow cytometry tests were carried out on CuS nanomaterial and dopamine@CuS/CS composite using neuronal SH-SY5Y, cervical derived L132 and lung A549 cells. It was found that the cell cycles and cellular viability were not changed demonstrating these carriers did not exhibit toxicity. Consequently, such light-responsive procedure was very effective in controlling and managing targeted medicine release particularly for the treatment of neurodegenerative syndromes.

PEG/PLGA nanoparticles were used to co-encapsulate methotrexate and Trapoxin A (MTX and TPX) anticancer pharmaceuticals to synergistically affect MCF-7 breast sarcoma cells [153]. The drug laden PEG/PLGA nanoparticles revealed a spherical shape and uniform nanoparticle size distribution. Moreover, mechanisms of cellular growth inhibition, cell cycle distribution and apoptotic cell decease stimulation were studied. MTX/TPX co-encapsulated PEG/PLGA nanoparticles synergistically illustrated anticancer activities and activating mitochondrial apoptosis path were detected in MCF-7 breast cancer cells. As well, these co-incorporated carriers significantly changed Bcl-2 and cyclin D1 expressions relative to pure MTX/TPX and each isolated medicine carrier. Hence, MTX/TPX co-encapsulated PEG/PLGA nanoparticles increased the breast cancer treatment efficacy.

The synthetic polymers and dendrimers are widely used as drug delivery vehicles and among these materials, dendrimers are very attractive because of their exceptional structures and properties [154]. The dendrimers are synthetic polymers having three-dimensional, well-defined and extremely branched structures. The dendrimer term was referred to the Greek word dendron which means tree denoting their distinctive tree-like branched structures [155]. All dendrimers are composed of three segments including (i) a central core having two or further reactive functional moieties, (ii) inner layers (every layer is known as a generation, G) containing repeating branched units that are bound onto the core by covalent bonds and (iii) terminal moieties existing over exterior surface. The synthesis of dendrimers is done by iterative stepwise coupling processes in both convergent and divergent manners. Polypropylenimine was the initial dendrimer-resembling material having little generations which was prepared in 1978 and after that, other dendrimers with greater generations and well-known structures were synthesized in the middle of 1980s [156].

The dendrimers have been employed as auspicious drug delivery carriers since they were prepared because they have plentiful surface functionalities and internal cavities [157]. The drug molecules are both encapsulated physically to their inner cavities and conjugated chemically onto their terminal functional moieties. The initial dendrimer used for drug encapsulation was done at mid-1990s using the dendritic box concept [158]. Later, various insoluble medications were encapsulated to dendrimers interiors in order to achieve effective DDSs. Nowadays, numerous drug delivery vehicles exist based on several commercially available dendrimers like poly(l-lysine) (PLL), poly(propylene imine) (PPI) and poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) that have been approved for the clinical applications. For instance, DEP® that is a conjugate of docetaxel with PEGylated PLL dendrimer, is firstly established by Starpharma Holdings Ltd. to treat various solid tumors such as breast, prostate and lung. It now undergoes phase I clinical evaluations. Furthermore, a sulfonated PLL dendrimer (VivaGel®) introduced by this company undergoes the clinical trials as a microbicidal compound to treat/prevent sexually transmitted diseases [159].

Recently, four types of pluronics including F127, P123, F108 and F68 were attached to the fourth generation of the dendrimer PAMAM [160]. The molecular weights of four dendrimer-pluronics and their diameters were in the range 377.14–200.15 kDa and about 60–180 nm, respectively. The dendrimer nanocarrier linked to the extremely hydrophobic pluronic P123 displayed a greater drug loading efficiency (76.25%) than other pluronic dendrimers. The drug containing nanocarriers exhibited high antiproliferative activities against MCF-7 breast cancer cells. Thus, such highly lipophilic pluronics conjugated nanocarriers were promising for the delivery of hydrophobic drugs. Fig. 20 reveals that the fibroblast cells were proliferated and viable in presence of the G4.0-F127 and G4.0-P123 while they were died upon incubation with the PAMAM G4.0 after 24 and 48 h.

Using maltose functionalized poly(propylene imine) glycol dendrimers (Fig. 21 ) with the 4th generation is a favorable approach for treatment of leukemia [161]. Also, anticancer therapy using nucleoside analogue drugs like cytarabine (Ara-C) shows low efficiency because of drug resistance, ineffective uptake and drug accumulation in cancer cells where it is transformed to active triphosphate congener. The PPI cationic dendrimer forms complexes by nucleotide Ara-C triphosphate, Ara-CTP. Considering these information, the PPI glycol dendrimers were employed as DDSs to deliver activated cytarabine into cancer cells in order to overwhelm the limitations of drug metabolism. For this purpose, the HL-60 and 1301 leukema cells along with mononuclear cells of peripheral blood were tested. The apoptosis and cytotoxicity assessments displayed superior capacity of Ara-CTP complexed with dendrimer compared to Ara-CTP and Ara-C against the 1301 leukema cells. Moreover, increased cytotoxicity and uptake of Ara-CTP-dendrimer complexes against 1301 cells expressing human equilibrative nucleoside transporter, hENT1, established that such complexation was valuable for the chemotherapy of resistant acute lymphoblast leukemic cells showing low expression of hENT1.

pH and GSH dual-sensitive degradable nano-sponge materials were fabricated using β-CD attached hyper-crosslinked polymer as the DOX carriers and examined in pH and GSH stimulated DOX release [162]. The presence of amino and carboxylic moieties plus disulfide bonds caused pH and GSH responsiveness of the nano-sponge, respectively. DOX was simply loaded in three dimensional matrixes of the nano-sponge carriers via formation of non-inclusion/inclusion complexes so that a high 22.6% DOX loading efficiency was measured. In vitro DOX release was considerably enhanced to ~77.0% within acidic pH = 5 and cytosolic reducing (10 mM GSH) medium. Changes in shapes of DOX loaded nanosponge carriers within releasing milieu (10 mM GSH, pH = 5) were investigated through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Confocal microscopy data proved that DOX delivery and release to cytoplasm as well as nucleus of A549 cells were happened during 7 h. Nanosponge materials displayed little cytotoxicity up to 1000 μg mL−1 dosage and extraordinary antitumor performance. Thus, the pH and GSH dual-sensitive nanosponge carriers were desirable materials for tumor treatment by targeted transport as well as controlled intracellular medicine release.

Synthesis of poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) second generation dendrimer was performed using cystamine core and applied to polymerize poly(methylacrylate) (PMA) which produced PAMAM-b-PMA block copolymers indicating diverse polymerization degrees for the PMA block [163]. The block copolymers formed spherical and hexagonal/cubic nanostructured supramolecules in water through self-assembly. Also, the DOX release experiments were done using these supramolecular nanostructured DDSs in various solutions. Greater drug loading efficiency was observed for block copolymers of shorter hydrophobic block length because strong interactions occurred among dendrimer chains and DOX molecules. Larger accumulative DOX release was measured for longer hydrophobic block size because of hydrophobic interactions among DOX molecules and PMA block. Furthermore, these DDSs exhibited substantial DOX release within acidic pH but any notable release was not detected at pH = 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proteins, peptides, phospholipids, micelles, liposomes and nucleic acids
ce:section.ce:para: Preparing macromolecules like proteins and peptides as the drug materials to treat various diseases is one of the most important areas in the pharmaceutical manufacturing [164,165]. In comparison to the common low molecular weight materials with MW < 500 g mol−1, proteins and peptides are more selective because their structures are rich with different functional groups allowing numerous interactions with the targets. The enhanced selectivity generally causes improved therapeutic influence and rather less side effects.

Among various proteins, those forming nanocages are very interesting. Most of protein nanocages like heat shock small proteins, ferritins and vaults are self-assembled in organisms such as Escherichia coli bacterium, mammalian cells and plants [166]. They have biological activities in living cells (e.g., iron homeostasis through storing extra iron ions in their cores, products catalysis in glucose metabolism, DNA protection from oxidative damage). They create extremely hierarchical structures with different shapes and sizes. The structures of cage proteins are composed of three segments including inner, outer and subunit parts. The inner cavity, called core, loads therapeutics and the outer surface can be utilized to protect the therapeutics and modified by functional ligands having targeting and biodistribution improving effects. The interactions in the subunit open the pores and promote cage reassembly/disassembly to allow the therapeutics encapsulation. All the characteristics prove that the protein nanocages are interesting drug carriers [167].

It is known that injectable hydrogels have various applications like controlled drug delivery, tissue engineering and regenerative medicine. In this context, a pure protein hydrogel was prepared using tetrameric ULD-GGGWRESAI and ULD-TIP1recombinant proteins by specific interactions among peptide and protein (Fig. 22 ) [168]. The toxicity assay in vitro designated that the hydrogel protein did not have any obvious cytotoxicity to HCEC and L-929 cells in 48 h. The protein hydrogel degradation was slowly occurred in phosphate buffer solution, pH 7.4, in 144 h. The sodium diclofenac drug was incorporated into the hydrogel and the drug release was nearly entirely happened from the hydrogel in 24 h through diffusion method. Thus, the protein hydrogel was found as a promising system for drug delivery purpose.

Nanomedicines are valuable materials which can deliver anticancer pharmaceutics with less invasive alternate treatments to brain tumors [169]. In this context, whey protein carrier nanomaterials were fabricated for targeted medication transport towards glioblastoma tumor cells via increasing drug distribution/bioavailability and decreasing body reaction to medicine resistance. Aluminum phthalocyanine chloride as a hydrophobic drug was chosen to be encapsulated into the whey protein carriers using spray drying procedure which indicated nanoparticle sizes of 100–300 nm. It was shown that irradiation to the drug incorporated DDS photodamaged the neoplasic cells and acted as an efficient method in malignant glioma therapy.

Cell penetrating peptides, CPPs, are one kind of peptides that effectively transport numerous materials such as macromolecules to cells in vitro and in vivo [170,171]. The CPPs are usually amphipathic and cationic peptides which are comprised of 4–40 amino acids. They can primarily be uptaken by cells through energy dependent paths by means of vesicles, particularly interact with cargos, although they were initially intended to pass plasma membranes directly by translocation [170,172]. The CPPs have widely been applied as auspicious delivery carriers to deliver bioactive compounds like nucleic acids, proteins and peptides into cells. The octa-arginine peptide, R8 peptide: RRRRRRRR-NH2 and HIV-Tat (48–60), TAT peptide: GRKKRRQRRRPPQ-NH2 are the most important CPPs that could efficiently deliver bioactive agents to the cytoplasm through complex formation by bioactive compounds through non-covalent as well as covalent bonds. For instance, the TAT peptide transported the peptide nucleic acids to cells through the covalent linkages [173] but fatty acid functionalized R8 peptide transported the siRNA to cells by non-covalent attachments [174]. Furthermore, nanocapsule carriers like liposomes encapsulating bioactive agents were delivered to the cytoplasm by CPPs [175].

Delivering small interfering RNA, siRNA, to inhibit the glioblastoma growing is an obstacle because of the serious difficulties in crossing the blood-brain barrier and some siRNA properties including instability within serum and highly negative charges [176]. Some siRNA vehicles can be prepared from CPPs due to they have a great capacity to deliver oligonucleotide into the cells. Recently, two CPPs, amphipathic peptide PepFect 28 (PF28) and PepFect 14 (PF14), were functionalized by target peptides through covalent bonds and non-covalent complexation in order to increase specificity to glioma targeting as well as the gene-silencing efficacy. It was found that the non-covalent complex carrier (PF14:TG1) was effective to deliver siRNA into human U87glioblastoma cells displaying a substantial two times enhancement of gene silencing efficacy in comparison to the parent PF14 peptide and superior selectivity to the U87 cells relative to the targeting non-glioma cells.

6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) was attached onto a short peptide terminated by naphthylacetic acid (NF) through disulfide bonds and NF-SS-MP prodrug was formed to treat autoimmune and leukemia syndromes [177]. NF-SS-MP was self-assembled as nanofibrous structures of ~50 nm diameters within neutral water. Controlled and dependent in vitro medicine release was observed for NF-6-MP had GSH. Cytotoxicity assays after 4 and 24 h proved that 6-MP encapsulation into NF-SS-MP nanofiberous material enhanced the tumor cells death at early incubation times and considerably decreased 6-MP toxicity towards normal cells in 24 h. Cell uptake assays verified that NF-6-MP more easily absorbed into EC109 cells than pure 6-MP and showed greater intracellular drug amount in 4 h. Accordingly, the NF-SS-MP was an excellent 6-MP carrier which was effective in tumor therapy.

Liposomes are valuable drug platforms explored in 1960s [178]. They are composed of phospholipids that are constituents of the cell and biological membranes; thus they are very biocompatible delivery systems. Furthermore, liposomes contain both hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups that enable them to encapsulate hydrophobic and hydrophilic therapeutics. Therefore, they are able to deliver both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs in their hydrophobic bilayer coronas and aqueous cores, respectively. Moreover, they are simply modified chemically; hence, they are auspicious drug carriers that are effectively used in medical applications.

Structurally, liposomes are synthetic, enclosed phospholipid bilayers that are created from various phospholipids and generally stabilized with the large cholesterol molar segments [179]. Preparation of liposomes from these lipid formulations enables durable drug encapsulation and loading depending on the chemical properties of liposomes like pH-sensitivity, molecular weight and hydrophilicity. Liposomes can be obtained as very homogenous nanoparticles with very small particle sizes which can be beneficial for their therapeutic efficiency. As synthetic liposomes are created through self-assembly, they can encapsulate nearly big hydrophilic pharmaceutics. They are modified by targeted ligands to achieve numerous drug-loaded nanoparticles with different sizes and functionalized surfaces to be specifically accumulated in disease sites with improved uptakes by cells expressing the receptors for the attached ligands [180].

It has been recognized that image directed delivery of drugs by means of imageable thermosensitive liposomes, iTSLs, in addition to the high intensity focused ultrasound, HIFU or FUS, is a non-invasive method to deliver targeted anticancer agents [180]. Also, the FUS triggered hyperthermia can be applied as an external stimulus to release drugs from a thermosensitive carrier (Fig. 23 ).
