The sub-ablative hyperthermia can considerably modify tumor vasculature permeability and increase uptake of nanoparticles. Hence, near infrared fluorescence, NIRF, and magnetic resonance imaging, MRI, labeled thermosensitive liposomes were prepared and utilized for the biodistribution imaging/tracking and the drug transport to cancer bearing mouse. The iTSLs were encapsulated by Hycamtin® (topotecan) which is a chemotherapeutic compound and once it is released into tumors the intrinsic drug fluorescence will be increased. The FUS method was employed by feedback through subcutaneously located fine-wire thermocouples in order to preserve and display the hyperthermic temperatures. The iTSL accumulation was immediately occurred in tumors by means of NIRF imaging after administration of liposome. It was shown that FUS triggered mild hyperthermia for 3 min at 42 °C and 30 min after intravenous injection significantly increased uptake of the iTSLs. A co-localized increase in the fluorescence emission of the topotecan was immediately measured subsequent to employing the FUS which indicated a triggered fast drug release. The improved iTSL accumulation and simultaneous topotecan release were intensified through another mild hyperthermia application 1 h later. The in vivo MRI established superior iTSLs uptake by the FUS treatment. Therefore, the imaging data proved that the hyperthermia affected the uptake of both drug and carriers. Also, the FUS triggered hyperthermia accompanied by real-time imaging is a valuable technique for tumor targeted drug delivery.

Among diverse categories of clinically used nano-formulations, the lipid-based nanocarriers including lipid NPs, liposomes, nanoemulsions and nanocapsules have the prevalent role. Several platforms utilize such lipid-based systems for the delivery of various biologically active agents because they have many advantages including their least toxicity for in vivo uses [181]. Usually, these systems are biocompatible, biodegradable, non-immunogenic and simply formulated. In fact, they have been long employed to change properties of drugs to increase their therapeutic indices through adjusting their uptake, dropping toxicity, decreasing metabolism and enhancing their biological half-lives. It is noteworthy that there are a number of drugs having low water solubility and thereby with limited oral absorption. The lipid-based systems particularly self-emulsifying formulations are effectively used to offer efficient oral delivery approaches for these molecules [182].

Gadodiamide (Gd-DTPA-BMA) is a Gd containing complex and that is applied for MRI as contrast agent and neoplastic cells apoptosis as anticancer medicine but it exhibited little cell internalization. Thus, thermosensitive liposomes were used as carriers to enhance its antitumor effectiveness [183]. These formulations were obtained via hydration of lipid films. Cytotoxicity capacity and selectivity of thermosensitive liposomes composed of Gd-DTPA-BMA were studied. WI-26 VA4 normal and 4T1/MDA-MB-231 tumor cells were employed to estimate cellular viability. These lipid compositions were appropriate as Lβ→Lα transition temperatures were close to those achieved for thermosensitive liposomes and employed in cancer therapy. The Gd-DTPA-BMA incorporated liposomes revealed greater cytotoxicity towards breast sarcoma cells compared to pure Gd-DTPA-BMA. Furthermore, liposomes illustrated minimum cytotoxicity to normal cells than pure Gd-DTPA-BMA. Consequently, thermosensitive liposomes comprising Gd-DTPA-BMA were auspicious nanocarriers in breast cancer therapy.

Phospholipids are key components in all kinds of cell membranes that possess amphiphilic property which create lipid bilayers [182]. The phospholipids structures contain two fatty acid hydrophobic tails plus a phosphate hydrophilic head that have been linked together through alcohol or glycerol. The hydrophilic head is interacted with water and consists of glycerol and phosphate with negative charges but the hydrophobic tails include two long chains of fatty acids having repulsion with water that are enforced to be aggregated. The phospholipids form various structures in water depending on their particular features. These specific characteristics determine their important roles in the lipid bilayers. In biological organizations, they are commonly created along with other molecules such as glycolipids, proteins and sterols in bilayers like cell membranes. The lipid bilayers form once the hydrophobic tails array parallel with each other to produce a membrane with hydrophilic heads are placed in two sides which are faced with water molecules. The difference of tail and head groups, alcohols and aliphatic chains creates numerous phospholipids. Depending on the alcohols existing in the phospholipids, they are classified as glycerophospholipids and sphingomyelins. The eukaryotic cells contain glycerophospholipids with glycerol backbones are known as their key phospholipids. The most important part of cell membranes in animals possesses sphingomyelins with sphingosine backbones that affect the lipid bilayers formation [184].

A phospholipid complex is prepared from the interactions of phospholipids with drug molecules in a non-protonic solvent [185]. It was proved that phospholipid complexes are able to increase the solubility or lipophilicity of various pharmaceuticals (e.g., ellagic acid, silybin, berberine and rifampicin) thereby improving the oral bioavailability. A nanoemulsion composed of phospholipid and drug complex was achieved as a delivery vehicle using the advantages of both nanoemulsions and phospholipid-drug complexes [186]. An evodiamine-phospholipid nanoemulsion was also developed indicating superior bioavailability and absorption. A complex of phospholipid and matrine improved matrine lipophilicity, loaded more drug molecules to the oily phase in the self-nanoemulsion and enhanced the drug bioavailiability [187].

It is known that dabigatran etexilate, DE, is not soluble in neutral pH solutions however it becomes soluble in low pH media upon protonation and this is the main reason for low bioavailability by the DE commercial formulations [188]. To solve this problem, a DE nanoemulsion system was prepared exhibiting enhanced solubility within the simulated intestinal fluid after encapsulation of DE to the oily phase however 35.8% of drug was not loaded. Additionally, a DE-phospholipid complex was produced to improve both solubility and lipophilicity for the DE. Nanoemulsions of phospholipid and DE considerably decreased the drug leakage and succeeding precipitation. The nearly bioavailability for the nanoemulsions of phospholipid and DE was enhanced to 606.6 and 147.3% relative to commercial DE products and DE nanoemulsions, respectively. Accordingly, drug-phospholipid complex nanoemulsion effectively improved oral bioavailability of pH-dependent soluble drugs.

A phospholipid micelle nanomaterial containing olmesartan medoxomil (OLM) and surface decorated by sodium deoxycholate (bile salt) penetrating enhancer was prepared to improve targeted lymphatic drug transport and enhance systemic bioavailability [189]. The optimized phospholipid micelle nanoformulation displayed 168 nm diameter, −22.4 mV zeta potential and 70% medicine loading capacity. It was indicated that 70% OLM release was happened in 12 h in a sustained manner but nearly 100% OLM release in 24 h. Caco-2 cells were cultured onto the micelles and >90% viability and OLM uptake were observed after 6 h. Pharmacokinetic studies using rat plasma showed approximately 6.02 and 10.62 times greater Cmax and AUC0-t for OLM released from micelle nanoformulation compared to the OLM alone. However, lymphatic uptake experiments on rats illustrated almost 3.04 folds enhancement of OLM level within lymph released from the micelle nanoformulation compared to the OLM alone after 24 h. The intestinal non-toxicity of the micelle nanoformulation was evaluated using histopathology assay indicating its biocompatibility with no cytotoxicity to this tissue. Hence, the in vitro and in vivo data verified that the phospholipid micelle nanoformulation was a valuable candidate to enhance drug oral bioavailability which controlled hypertension.

Micelles are composed of core-shell structures which can be produced using amphiphilic block copolymers through self-assembly in an aqueous environment. They can very effectively transport bioactive agents, drugs and proteins because they mimic the naturally occurring delivery systems [190]. Poly(ethylene glycol)–block-poly(lactic acid) copolymer, PEG–b-PLA, in aqueous solution creates spherical micelles through self-assembly that are composed of PLA-cores and PEG-coronas. A hydrophobic drug is simply loaded to the hydrophobic core but the hydrophilic corona prolongs the circulation time [191]. The PEG–b-PLA micelles have widely been used to deliver hydrophobic drugs like doxorubicin and paclitaxel proving their capacity as drug controlled delivery systems. Nevertheless, the most common drawback of peritumoral and intratumoral administration of such nanoformulations is fast dispersion of drug molecules to the neighboring normal tissues thereby decreasing the drug therapeutic efficacy. In order to solve this problem, injectable hydrogels can be prepared and used as drug carriers, thus the drug is delivered to the disease areas in a non-invasive manner devoid of surgical harms. For instance, supramolecular hydrogels created by the host-guest interactions between cyclodextrins and guest molecules could be employed as injectable and controllable drug delivery platforms.

Filomicelles with folic acid (FA) targeting group highly maximizes treatment effectiveness but minimizes side effects. Influences of FA-functionalized PLA micelles compositions were examined onto drug loading and release amounts plus cytotoxic capacity towards cancer cells [192]. E−29-diethoxyphosphoryl-28-O-propynoylbetulin was used as the anticancer pharmaceutical incorporated to the micelle DDSs. Bare micelles exhibited in vitro biocompatibility and the drug encapsulated micelles indicated toxicity to FR-positive SK-BR-3 cells. The micelle carriers displayed drug release in >260 h, though they had different shapes, drug encapsulation and release characteristics. It was shown that length of hydrophobic block controlled intra- and inter-molecular interactions as well as micelles features like encapsulation capacities and release rates.

Co-delivery of a chemotherapeutic agent and a therapeutic small interfering RNA (siRNA) to cancer cells is auspicious in cancer therapy and offers improved cell killing and low side effects [193]. In this context, cholesterol-modified chitosan (C-CS) was applied as the self-assembled transport vehicle for the two curcumin (a chemotherapeutic hydrophobic drug) and siRNA into tumor cells. It was found that the curcumin/siRNA incorporated (C-CS/siRNA) nanoparticles exhibited a zeta potential of +24.8 ± 2.2 mV and an average particle size of 165 ± 2.6 nm. The C-CS/siRNA revealed high stability at 4 °C during more than one month. The optimum C-CS condensation by the siRNA was happened using the lowest N/P ratio that was 40. The C-CS/siRNA was well uptaken by the human lung A549 carcinoma cells and this was a time dependent process. The cellular C-CS/siRNA internalization was detected by clathrin-mediated endocytosis (Fig. 24 ). Thus, conjugates of cholesterol and chitosan could be used as co-delivery systems for both hydrophobic drugs and siRNA in combination cancer treatment.

The rapid advances in the arena of DNA nanotechnology have led to various methods for the preparation of nucleic acid nanostructures with precise shapes, sizes and periodicities in one, two and three dimensions [194]. Consequently, these nanostructures are interesting in nanomedicine where multifunctional nano-scaled assemblies are instantly necessary. The DNA nano-objects can completely be self-assembled using nucleic acids through Watson-Crick pairing of bases and they are functionalized via hybridization by the oligonucleotides. For instance, an icosahedron DNA functionalized by a cell distinguishing aptamer target and a DNA tube with 140 nm length were incorporated by anticancer pharmaceutics [195]. The DNA strands are chemically bound onto an inorganic nanoscopic template and simply modified by drug molecules/targeted groups through hybridization with complementary oligonucleotide conjugates. Recently, the inorganic material was substituted with a core soft material composed of hydrophobic polymeric chains linked to oligonucleotides by covalent bonds and formed block copolymers of DNA [196]. The DNA nanocarriers are mostly utilized for cancer treatment.

The interaction of double stranded DNA with aluminum phthalocyanine chloride (AlClPc) was studied by resonance light scattering, absorption, fluorescence and circular dichroism spectra in water and water/ethanol mixtures containing diverse concentrations of AlClPc and DNA [197]. The AlClPc was efficiently bound to preferentially to the grooves of DNA in both water and water/ethanol mixture as its monomeric and aggregated original forms, respectively, so that the monomeric or aggregate state was dependent to the AlClPc:DNA ratio. At low AlClPc concentrations, the AlClPc interaction with few DNA sites created a curvature in the DNA structure that was an auspicious milieu to intercalate the AlClPc aggregates. Also, increasing the AlClPc concentration led to increasing the interactions with numerous binding sites on DNA, which stopped bending and aggregation of AlClPc in the double-stranded DNA. Accordingly, this approach could be used for clinical applications in photodynamic therapy to treat oral/skin cancer and scars/wounds.

It was shown that nucleic acids were attractive materials to construct nano-substances as drug carriers because multifunctional NPs could indicate crucial effects [194]. In fact, the DNA nanotechnology was utilized to treat ophthalmic diseases because eye drops exhibited low effectiveness due to they had short survival times over the eye surface. Consequently, such topical ocular therapy necessitates administrating high and frequent doses of drug which usually does not lead to good bioavailability. Fortunately, the DNA nanocarrier system solved this problem so that lipid-functionalized non-toxic DNA strands (12mers, four lipid functionalized thymines at the 50 end) formed identical micelle NPs that were adhered onto the corneal surface in prolonged time periods. Upon one-step self-assembly, DNA strands were hybridized with an aptamer and encapsulated diverse drugs. These DNA NPs demonstrated extended survival times which led to their enhanced effectiveness. Also, they revealed good performance in some ex vivo and in in vivo animal tests. Thus, it was established that such NPs were also suitable even for usage in human tissues.

In another work, a lipid-DNA (UU11mer) micelle containing two hydrophobic alkyl chains was fabricated as a drug carrier which maximized the effectiveness and decreased unwanted effects of budesonide hydrophobic drug [198]. Budesonide is known as a highly anti-inflammatory glucocorticoid medicine used in treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease and asthma. Great budesonide loading capacity was measured for the lipid-DNA drug carrier. The cellular assay on the inhibited release of interleukin-8 (IL-8) from this carrier proved that the drug preserved its anti-inflammatory potential. Therefore, it was suggested that such formulation indicating highly improved bioavailability and water solubility of the drug could be used for the non-invasive budesonide administration.

Erythromycin antibiotic is frequently employed nonetheless body resistance to this antineoplastic drug results in low drug effectiveness [199]. Hence, three-dimensional tetrahedral framework nucleic acid (tFNA) nanostructures were applied to enhance its antibacterial activity and stability towards Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacterium. The tFNA vehicles augmented the erythromycin uptake by bacteria and assisted the bacterial membranes damage. Besides, tFNA improved permeability into the bacteria cell walls and decreased drug resistance via increasing drug movements through the membranes. The erythromycin loaded tFNAs vehicles were efficient materials that could target antibiotic-resistant bacterial species of thick cell walls.

Photosensitizer materials are crucially utilized in photodynamic therapy but most of them indicated low selectivity and needed to be targeted using carrier nanostructures [200]. Accordingly, aptamer-guided DNA tetrahedra were developed as platforms for targeted transport of some photosensitizers. The sequence of Mucin 1 protein aptamer (MUC1) overexpressing in several cancer cells was bound onto a DNA tetrahedron and MUC1 aptamer functionalized tetrahedra nanomaterials (MUC1-TDNs) were achieved and used to target MUC1-positive tumor cells. The TMPyP4 photosensitizer was incorporated in MUC1-TDNs because of its high affinity to DNA and a targeted photosensitizer DDS was attained, i.e. MUC1-TDNs-TMPyP4 which could selectively deliver TMPyP4 into MUC1-positive cancer cells. Additionally, MUC1-TDNs-TMPyP4 produced ROS and led to toxicity within MUC1-positive cells but lower killing influence was detected within MUC1-negative cells. Hence, MUC1 targeted photosensitizer carriers were fabricated using DNA tetrahedra that revealed targeted photosensitizer release.

A nanocarrier was achieved using linear DNA nanomaterial (LDN) that effectively co-delivered epirubicin (Epi) anticancer medicine plus antimiR-10b oligonucleotide drug to 4T1 and MCF-7 breast cancer cells [201]. The LDN with high serum stability contained AS1411 targeting aptamer, therapeutic FOXM1 Apt aptamer as well as complementary linker strand. The Epi encapsulated LDN extraordinarily internalized targeted nucleolin positive 4T1 and MCF-7 cells compared to non-targeted nucleolin negative CHO cells. The Epi incorporated LDN considerably diminished 4T1 and MCF-7 cells viability whereas it exhibited lower toxicity to non-targeted cells confirming its targeting capacity. Additionally, the Epi incorporated LDN considerably more effective inhibited tumor growth in mice compared to pure LDN and Epi confirming combination treatment was very substantial in tumor therapy.

It has been well recognized that photodynamic therapy is applied as the clinical method in chemotherapy of diverse sarcomas and DNA compartment nanostructures containing G-quadruplex are valuable vehicles for cationic porphyrin photosensitizer materials particularly in combination with cellular targeted aptamers [202]. So far, little research has been done to identify characteristics of complexes formed between aptamer and G-quadruplex. Such complexes are appropriate materials to capsulate photosensitizer compounds and targeted cell recognitions. Recently, tetramolecular, bimolecular and unimolecular (TM, BM UM, respectively) G-quadruplex architectures were employed as drug incorporating parts that were bound to tumor cells targeting Sgc8 aptamer to construct some targeted DNA vehicles called T-GMVs for TMPyP4 medicine. It was shown that merely TM G-quadruplex was appropriate for combination Sgc8 to fabricate drug carrier and TMPyP4/T-GMV complex displayed anticipated anticancer efficiency. Thus, TMPyP4/T-GMV was introduced as a promising material to develop photosensitizer drug vehicles using G-quadruplex for application in targeted chemotherapy.

Controlled drug delivery systems are very attractive to target specific disease sites compared to other conventional chemotherapeutics, gene therapy and siRNA technology [203]. Smart drug nanocarriers are usually composed of functional units including porous carriers, targeted ligands, tracking markers and gate keepers. The gate keepers are typically created in controlled drug release to respond external or internal triggers. The internal stimulus (such as redox, enzyme and pH stimuli) systems can release the drug to respond physicochemical features of sarcoma cells whereas external trigger (such as magnetic field and hyperthermia) vehicles release their loaded therapeutics to respond an external stimulus [204].

The stimuli-responsive materials have been produced to achieve efficient and controlled drug release through sensing the cellular environment [205]. Numerous stimuli-responsive mechanisms have been offered including pH, temperature, redox, light, magnetic, electrochemical potential, glucose, enzyme, microwave, ultrasonic and ionic strength. It is notable that the pH-responsive drug release is frequently employed because the pH amount of the normal tissues (pH 7.4) is different from those of extracellular acidic lysosomes with pH 4–5, tumor medium with pH < 6.5 and endosomes with pH 5–6 [206]. In general, two approaches are used to specifically trigger the drug release through the pH gradient including conjugation of drug to a nanocarrier containing an acid-labile bond such as orthoester, imine, hydrazone and phosphoramidate that is hydrolyzed to release the drugs as well as creation of a system having ionizable groups that result in dissolution or conformational variations to respond diverse pH values. The hydrazone chemistry is extensively applied in biomedicine due to it displays stability upon blood circulation however it is quickly hydrolyzed when encountered to endo/lysosomal pH environments. Furthermore, the cells have a vital non-protein thiol called glutathione (GSH) which is very significant to regulate cellular processes like cell differentiation, proliferation and apoptosis [207]. The GSH concentration in tumor cells is ~2–10 mM which is at least four times greater than that of the normal cells. Considering these facts, smart DDSs are often developed as biological-stimuli responsive vehicles.

Thus far, numerous smart pH responsive DDSs have been designed and prepared. The controlled pH-responsive drug release is commonly adjusted through on and off modes by the pores by their gatekeepers [206]. Recently, biodegradable and biocompatible polyurethanes (PUs) were achieved using various amino acids as chain extenders [208]. The PUs were screened for their in vivo and in vitro cytotoxicity, swelling, water uptake and hydrolytic degradation and used as pH-responsive drug carriers.

Among several pH-responsive drug delivery systems, mesoporous silica nanoparticles, MSNs, are significantly interesting in effective delivery of drugs because they have shown outstanding drug loading capabilities. For instance, a pH-responsive smart drug release formulation was prepared by means of MSNs as a CD44 targeted system [197]. Also, hyaluronic acid, HA, was served for end-capping and coverage of drug loaded within the MSNs mesopores by the acid-labile bonds of hydrazine. The HA located over the particles surfaces acted as a site specific targeted material to enable targeting to the cells overexpressing CD44 (Fig. 27 ). Such systems were satisfactory stable in physiological pH values and the drug release was triggered in response to the pH variation. The hyaluronic acid functionalized particles revealed much improved cellular uptake through the endocytosis mediated by the CD44 receptor in CD44 receptor positive Hela cells; thus the doxorubicin-loaded MSNs demonstrated considerably superior drug effectiveness to the CD44 receptor overexpressing sarcoma cells, IC50 = 0.56 μg mL−1, but normal cells indicated poor cytotoxicity (IC50 = 1.03 μg mL−1). Results proved that the doxorubicin-loaded MSNs could be considered as high performance DDSs for cancer treatment [209].

In another work, pH-responsive intracellular NPs were obtained using hyaluronic acid-hydrazone-doxorubicin, HA-hyd-DOX, to be employed in acid-triggered release of doxorubicin, see Figs. 28 and 29
[210]. The amphiphilic polymeric prodrugs were self-assembled in aqueous solution to form nanoparticles with core-shell spherical morphology and uniform sizes in the range of 220 to 167 nm. HA-hyd-DOX was appropriately in vitro stable and DOX release was evidently controlled by the pH change. The cell counting kit-8 assay, CCK-8, established low cytotoxicity of HA-hyd-DOX to normal L929 mouse fibroblast cells and high antitumor activity to HeLa human cervical cancer cells through the drug release in response to the intracellular pH value. The flow cytometric profiles and confocal laser scanning microscopy images displayed efficient uptake by the HeLa cells which was related to the facilitated highly specific affinity of the CD44 receptor to HA confirming this smart pH sensitive drug delivery system could be utilized to design potential anticancer prodrugs for the cancer therapy.

A nano-hydrogel carrier was synthesized and used in cisplatin targeted transportation [211]. The nano-hydrogel was synthesized through randomly co-polymerized aminoethyl methacrylamide using polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate crosslinker. Also, the nano-hydrogel was attached onto l-histidine. After encapsulation of cisplatin into the nano-hydrogel, its conjugate with tetraiodothyroacetic acid was prepared. Amounts of cisplatin loaded and released from the nanocarrier were measured. Also, the nano-hydrogel toxicity was assessed using HT29 and HEK293 cells. The nano-hydrogels bioavailability in plasma of Sprague Dawley rats was estimated and pharmacokinetic results were achieved. This nano-hydrogel was a pH responsive chemotherapy material while tumor microenvironments varied and greatly targeted HT29 cancer cells more effectively compared to pure cisplatin. Hence, such nano-hydrogel formulation indicating nanosize diameter, rather desirable hemo-compatibility, pH responsivity, active targeting through integrin receptors and improved pharmacokinetics was an attractive chemotherapy agent.

To enhance the therapeutic effects of antibiotics against infective illnesses and to decrease microbial resistances to drugs, pH sensitive hybrid GAPAH-LPH polymer/lipid nanostructures were developed via microemulsion process for co-delivery of 18β-glycyrrhetinic acid (GA) and vancomycin (VCM) antibiotic [212]. GAPAH-LPH/VCM revealed encapsulation efficiency of 69.46 ± 2.52%, zeta potential of −3.8 ± 0.335 mV, polydispersity index of 0.255 ± 0.003 and diameter of 198.4 ± 0.302 nm. Quicker and slower VCM release in vitro was measured at acidic pH than pure VCM. GAPAH-LPH/VCM showed 16 folds superior in vitro bactericidal activity than the pure VCM towards Staphylococcus aureus that was resistant to methicillin. As well, GAPAH-LPH/VCM eliminated biofilm as established using crystal violet assessment. Consequently, the GAPAH-LPH/VCM was a favorable antibiotic nanocarrier which enhancd microbicidal effectiveness.

Temperature responsive drug delivery systems release the loaded therapeutics in response to the changes in temperature. Some temperature responsive nanothin hydrogel films were developed using poly(N-vinylcaprolactam) (mPVCL) NPs of extraordinarily high loading ability to be applied a topical drug carriage [213]. Greatly swolled (mPVCL)n multilayered hydrogels, with n represents the nanoparticle layer number, were created through layer by layer assembly by the hydrogen bonding interactions among PVCL-co-acrylic acid core-shell NPs and linear poly(N-vinylpyrrolidone) and subsequent acrylic acid shell crosslinking by adipic acid dihydrazide or ethylene diamine.

It was found that the (mPVCL)5 film could undergo reversible high swelling at pH 7.5 that was 9 times greater than its dry form demonstrating 89 v/v% of water existed within the hydrogel matrix. The hydrogels displayed extremely reversible variations of ~3-fold in their thicknesses when temperature was changed between 25 and 50 °C at the normal human skin pH = 5. The hydrogel (mPVCL)30 containing ~120 mg cm−2 sodium diclofenac medicine (an anti-inflammatory non-steroidal drug employed in osteoarthritis pain treatment) illustrated that the drug was sustainably permeated to an artificial skin membrane in 24 h at 32 °C (average temperature of human skin surface), Fig. 30 . The total diclofenac quantity released from the hydrogel (mPVCL)30 in 24 h at 32 °C was 12 times greater compared to what measured at 22 °C indicating the temperature sensitivity of the hydrogel.

A pH and temperature dual stimuli responsive carrier was produced using polymer covered MSNs to facilitate photothermal therapy and chemotherapy [214]. The grafting of the pH and temperature dual responsive poly((N-isopropylacrylamide-co-methacrylic acid), NIPAM-co MA, to the MSNs was performed through polymerization in situ and then doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) as a chemotherapeutic drug and indocyanine green (ICG) as a NIR absorber phototherapeutic were loaded into the polymer coated MSNs to achieve the smart drug delivery vehicle called DOX-ICG-MSN@p(NIPAM-co-MA). Under the NIR irradiation, the ICG photothermal conversion ability increased the carrier temperature and opened the gate through the p(NIPAM-co-MA) copolymer shrinkage in order to trigger controlled DOX release at higher temperature. Moreover, the drug release was happened at pH 5.3 which was the characteristic value in microenvironments of sarcoma cells that was due to the shrinkage by the pH-sensitive methacrylic acid polymeric fragment in the MSN@p(NIPAM-co-MA) and decreasing electrostatic interactions by the DOX molecules existing within the mesoporous channels via the silanol groups protonation. Also, the ICG more warranted photodynamic and photothermal therapy. The drug endocytosis was confirmed by cell imaging. The cytotoxicity test using HeLa cells showed that the combined usage of ICG and DOX led to an evident synergistic influence by because they were more efficient in killing HeLa cells than free ICG and DOX (Fig. 31 ).

In another work, magnetic hydrogel (Mag-H) composite was prepared using gelatin and bacterial cellulose, BC, matrices incorporated with gelatin covered magnetic NPs that were crosslinked by glutaraldehyde [215]. The drug release was examined in vitro by means of methylene blue drug. The bacterial cellulose and magnetic NPs were loaded to the gelatin matrix to enhance mechanical and thermal features for the gelatin hydrogel, Gel-H. The superparamagnetic characteristics of magnetic NPs endowed with the Mag-H a strong magnetic character to perform magnetic responsive drug delivery. The Mag-H showed the highest swelling of 1085 ± 12% at 37 °C in PBS solution with pH 1.2; also, the greatest drug release, 80.77 ± 0.33%, was happened under identical conditions by applying external magnetic fields (Fig. 32 ). The Mag-H displayed magnetic field, temperature and pH responsive features that were much valuable in controlled and targeted drug delivery.

Smart temperature and pH dual sensitive carriers were developed based on phase change microcapsules (MEPCM) [216]. For this purpose, n-eicosane was used as PCM (phase change material) that was loaded into acrylate modified silica shells and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-acrylate acid) was coated onto the silica shell surface. The MEPCM with a core–shell structure exhibited homogeneous size distribution, spherical shape, >160 J/g thermal energy storage capacity non-leaking property, stability in thermal cycles and stable morphology at high temperatures. Also, it was found that this carrier exhibited pH and temperature sensitivities that independently acted their roles when released DOX and bovine serum albumin medicines. Hence, the MEPCM was valuable smart vehicle of pharmaceuticals.

Dual sensitive DOX carrier was achieved using MSNs prepared using rice husk to be applied in sarcoma therapy [217]. The MSNs nanostructures were modified with vinyl moieties, acrylic acid plus n-isopropyl acrylamide through polymerization onto their surfaces to obtain the vehicle. Then, encapsulation of DOX was done and DOX release in vitro was performed. Exceptional controllable DOX release was measured due to dual temperature as well as pH responsivity of the biocompatible carrier. Pure DOX and the DOX incorporated vehicle revealed cytotoxicity towards MCF-7 cells. Besides, the MCF-7 cells apoptosis was detected through morphology changes and flow cytometry. As a result, such dual temperature and pH sensitive nanovehicle with minor DOX release throughout blood circulation but fast DOX release at tumor tissue, was recognized as an especially beneficial chemotherapy agent.

Anticancer DDSs enhance water dissolution of hydrophobic medicines, extend their bioavailability and decrease the side effects to normal cells. Recently, a targeted, degradable and GSH and pH dual sensitive carrier was prepared based on dendritic (DMSNs) which loaded DOX pharmaceutical and hyaluronic acid (HA) targeting agent to produce DOX/HA/DMSNs [218]. The particle diameter of DOX/HA/DMSNs was in the range of 100–150 nm which indicated 283 mg g−1 DOX loading efficiency as they had uniform radial channels. The in vitro release from DOX/HA/DMSNs was observed in 5 days at 10 mM GSH and pH = 5.5 which confirmed outstanding GSH and pH sensitive properties of the carrier. The DOX/HA/DMSNs noticeably illustrated greater toxicity to MCF-7 tumor cells because it had both GSH and pH dual triggering activities but HA/DMSNs nanocarrier displayed less toxicity against MCF-7 cells. Additionally, flow cytometry proved that the DOX/HA/DMSNs had excellent targeted delivery towards MCF-7 cells than L929 cells that were HA-receptor positive and negative, respectively. Hence, it was suggested that the DOX/HA/DMSNs might have targeted tumor specific capacity and could be a promising material for chemotherapy.

High intracellular ROS concentration within cancerous cells can be considered as an anticancer stimulus which was used in cancer treatment [219]. Consequently, ROS sensitive medicine vehicles were achieved using poly(ethylene glycol)-poly(methionine), PEG-P(Met), and loaded by piperlongumine (PL) pro-oxidant hydrophobic pharmaceutic. In fact, the carriers formed core–shell nanosized self-assembled micelles that encapsulated PL in P(Met) core. Enhancement of ROS concentration in sarcoma cells stimulated changing hydrophobic property of polypeptide to hydrophilic and caused ROS sensitive micelles degradation and therefore effective PL release in cancerous cells. PL containing PEG-P(Met) micelles displayed more apoptosis of MCF-7 human breast cancer cells than pure PL because effectual intracellular PL release was happened. Also, the PEG-P(Met)/PL micelles demonstrated cancer specific toxicity to MCF-7 cells because of highly increased intracellular ROS concentration within these cells approving they could be potent chemotherapy materials.

ROS is an eminent vital factor which can initiate atherosclerosis creation leading to endothelial dysfunction plus plaque formation [220]. Numerous antithrombotic treatments apply ROS sensitive carriers indicating therapeutic effects to some extent; however, they are not able to remove extra ROS and consequently the atherosclerosis is not treated effectively. Besides, the immune system simply clears nanomaterials via blood circulation indicating they are effective in elongated times. Recently, smart ROS sensitive carrier was fabricated to respond shear stress of atherosclerotic plaques milieu. Such vehicle was obtained using simvastatin incorporated micelles (SVMC) and red blood cells (RBCs). Micelles were produced based on hydrophobic poly(glycidylmethacrylate)-polypropylene sulphide named PPS that reacted with extra ROS, became hydrophilic and forced micelle damage to release the medicine. Also, PPS considerably diminished the ROS concentration and caused synergistic atherosclerosis therapy using drug molecules. The electrostatic attractions between the negatively and positively charged RBCs and SVMC, respectively, resulted in their self-assembly which afforded SVMC@RBCs. The SVMC@RBCs responded to great shear stress of the atherosclerotic plaques which promoted separation of SVMC and RBC. Biomimetic procedures were used to avoid removal of SVMC@RBCs via immune system and ROS decrease which indicated improved in vivo and in vitro atherosclerosis therapy along with avoiding the bleeding risk. Accordingly, the SVMC@RBCs was an auspicious nanomaterial that could be applied in treatment of ROS associated disorders.

In another study, ROS and GSH dependent polymer dot (PD) was obtained as a mitochondria targeted material using triphenylphosphonium (TPP) and diselenide bond [221]. The fluorescence carrier was employed in cancer theranostics plus paclitaxel (PTX) delivery and release. TPP-PD vehicle improved stability of PTX loaded into the carrier and diselenide bond break down by H2O2 and GSH triggers which caused controlled drug release and greater fluorescence activity. Higher H2O2 and GSH concentrations in cancerous cells than normal cells resulted in selective PTX release to sarcoma cells, reduced cellular viability of only nearly 25% and increased cancerous cell apoptosis than normal cells. The fluorescence spectra proved that PTX/TPP-PD was selective to PC-3 and HeLa cancer cells in comparison to normal CHO–K1 cells. Additionally, TPP bonding stimulated PTX/TPP-PD specific deposition within mitochondria which allowed PTX release into mitochondria of sarcoma cells. Therefore, enhanced PTX therapeutic effectiveness along with minimized side effects to normal cells were witnessed using such drug nanocarrier.

Recently, ROS and redox sensitive MSNs vehicles were fabricated which respond to H2O2 as the ROS and also dithiothreitol as the redox reagent [222]. These DSSs contained four fragments including MSNs, selenocystine as ROS sensitive and dithiodipropionic as redox sensitive, hyaluronic acid as targeting and coating material as well as anticancer medicine. It was shown that >50% differences were measured for drug release in presence and absence of the ROS and redox sensitive agents. Besides, the drug release was dependent on the stimulus dosage and confirmed the smart characteristics of these nanocarriers within diverse environments.

Hypoxia indicating ischemia and leads to several illnesses like cancer because tumor hypoxia results in cancer [223]. MSNs nanomaterials with specific drug release in hypoxia environments were prepared using 4-(phenylazo)benzoic acid (4-PA, SNA) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) to obtain SNAC carrier. β-CD and 4-PA complex was formed via host-guest interaction, served as gatekeeper to lock MSNs pores and then selectively cleaved 4-PA azo bond through intracellular nitroreductases in hypoxia environment which allowed drug release. SNAC/DOX revealed little toxicity in normoxic areas which reflected inhibited DOX release from SNAC through β-CD and 4-PA. On the other hand, in hypoxia milieu, SNAC/DOX displayed high toxicity like pure DOX. Hence, hypoxia sensitive SNAC was an efficient material for cancer therapy as well as diseases occurred through ischemia.

Bone metastasis can lead to cancer associated decease and has remained uncured despite countless investigations [224]. Bone targeting nanovehicles may tackle problems of transporting therapeutics into metastatic bone via stimuli sensitive and controlled cargo release to enhance cancer therapy performance. The metastatic bone environment is hypoxic indicating application of hypoxia sensitive bone targeting polymer micelles is possible for efficient therapy of bone metastatic prostate sarcoma. Micelles were obtained by self-assembly of poly-l-lysine and PEG copolymer. Alendronate was used as the bone targeting drug and azobenzene as the hypoxia sensitive linker which caused great affinity towards metastatic bone and strong in vitro hypoxia response. It was exhibited that upon specific deposition within metastatic bones in vivo, micelles responded toward hypoxic bone metastasis and rapidly released the drug with suitable therapeutic dose. In fact, micelles suppressed tumor progress within bone and inhibited bone damage through preventing osteoclast action but promoted osteoblast act which achieved boosted therapeutic results as bone pain was relieved and survival period was extended. Consequently, such bone targeted and hypoxia sensitive vehicles were auspicious materials for bone metastasis treatment.

Glioblastoma is among the most malicious tumors as it does not indicate favorable healing by chemotherapy that is associated with its short circulation in blood, blood–brain-barrier and uncontrolled release of pharmaceutics [225]. A hypoxia degrading zwitterionic phosphorylcholine nanogel called HPMPC was fabricated and used in effective glioblastoma therapy. The HPMPC nanogel indicated homogeneous diameter, elongated circulation within blood, anti-fouling capacity within mouse serum and good biocompatibility. Besides, it efficiently passed the blood–brain-barrier without using targeting agents/stimuli because phosphorylcholine polymers mimicked cell membranes structures and resulted in extended nanogel deposition within glioblastoma cells. The HPMPC nanogel contained azobenzene based crosslinker that degraded under hypoxic conditions, collapsed the nanogel and quickly released the drug into hypoxic tumor cells. Hence, such HPMPC nanogel exhibited desirable tumor inhibiting influence agaisnt glioblastoma but minor side effects reflecting its capacity for treatment of different hypoxic related syndromes within central nervous system.

Hypoxic tumor environment and non-selective sonosensitizers delivery are known as the main problems preventive clinical sonodynamic therapy (SDT) application for tumor chemotherapy [226]. Recently, hypoxia sensitive nanovesicles, hVs, were developed as sonosensitizer DDSs which enhanced the SDT performance by selective drug release plus localized oxygenation within tumors. These nanovesicles contained compactly stacked MnFe2O4 nanoparticles, MFNPs, encapsulated to hypoxia sensitive amphiphilic polymeric membranes. δ-Aminolevulinic acid was incorporated within the hVs hollow cavities which promptly dissociated them to isolated particles inside hypoxic tumor environment, released the cargo and induced ROS generation by ultrasound irradiation. The released MFNPs catalytically generated O2, combated hypoxic tumor microenvironment and enhanced the SDT effectiveness. MFNPs were separated posttreatment and easily cleared from body by renal clearance that reduced durable cytotoxicity. In vitro and in vivo assays proved efficient inhibition of tumor growth by hVs facilitated SDT and confirmed its capacity for treatment of deeply located hypoxic tumors which cannot easily be healed by common photodynamic treatment.

Designing controlled drug delivery systems using photoresponsive materials is very attractive due to smart materials can vary their structural composition/conformation in response to the irradiated light [227]. It is noteworthy that compared to internal stimuli (pH, redox and enzyme) which may indicate great differences between diverse patients, application of external stimuli such as light for controlled drug delivery has numerous benefits including easy-to-manipulate in addition to exact control of treatment location and time. Nevertheless, several light responsive systems release loaded drugs upon ultraviolet and visible light irradiations indicating somewhat poor penetration depths in tissues that is not suitable for in vivo applications [228]. On the other hand, the NIR light with in the wavelength range of 650–900 nm displays high penetration depth in tissue because of its noticeably decreased absorption and scattering through the biological organs, therefore it is favored in the in vivo triggered drug delivery [229].

It is known in polymeric nanomedicine that light-responsive polymeric materials can be prepared using o-nitrobenzyl alcohol compounds [230]. Similarly, copolymers of polysaccharide and poly(o-nitrobenzyl acrylate), PNBA, were achieved as PNBA core/dextran shell NPs through the dextran-g-PNBA glycopolymers nanoprecipitation and evaporation, emulsion by means of alkynated dextran and azide functionalized PNBA [230]. The NPs were used as photo-responsive DDSs and the in vitro cytotoxicity assay was done by means of Caco-2 cells. Also, the nanoparticles photosensitive activity plus colloidal stability were increased when salts are added. The nanoparticles were biocompatible to the Caco-2 cells and 100% cell viability was perceived once cells were exposed onto the nanoparticles under UV-irradiation for 60 s.

Although during the last decades an increasing number of nanoparticulate drug carriers have been designed for cancer therapy, inadequate cellular uptake of the sarcoma cells as well as unwanted endosomal and lysosomal entrapping of the cell internalized therapeutics are main drawbacks for several drug delivery vehicles [231]. With the aim of solving this problem, a lipid raft sensitive and light controlled polymer medication was produced to effectively deliver cytosolic photosensitizers. The protoporphyrin IX, PpIX, photosensitizer was conjugated onto cholesterol/polyethylene glycol polymer to obtain amphiphilic Chol−PEG−PpIX drug which can form micelles within the aqueous solution (Fig. 33 ). The Chol−PEG−PpIX containing two hydrophobic fragments (PpIX, cholesterol) exhibited strong linkage into the plasma membrane and allowed high cell uptake. For a systemic medicine transport, the Chol−PEG−PpIX was attached to liposomes surfaces by in situ modification of membrane that noticeably inhibited nonspecific Chol−PEG−PpIX attachment onto the red blood cells throughout circulation. Moreover, the Chol−PEG−PpIX bound to liposomes demonstrated decreased liver uptake, superior in vivo fluorescence, extended release, and efficient antitumor activity through photodynamic treatment. Thus, this is an efficient direct method for the cytosolic delivery of photosensitizers which is promising in cancer therapy.

In another research, an intelligent DDS was achieved through capping DOX encapsulated hollow mesoporous CuS NPs, HMCuS NPs, by the superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, IONPs [232]. Upon irradiation of the NIR light, the HMCuS NPs simultaneously exploited advantages of photodynamic therapy (PDT) as well as photothermal therapy (PTT). The multi-functionalized IONPs gatekeepers by improved capping efficacy could perform zero early release and minimalize undesirable side effects throughout in vivo drug carriage. The hybrid metallic HMCuS/DOX@IONP-PEG nanoplatform had superior PTT influence because of combined plasmon resonances and higher heat generation capability. The IONP-caps were efficiently removed after the NIR triggered photo-hyperthermia as it weakened the coordination bonds of IONPs and HMCuS-NH2 confirming the on-demand controlled drug delivery by NIR trigger through temporal and spatial resolutions. In addition to beneficial magnetic cancer targeting efficiency, in vivo and in vitro tests revealed an outstanding antitumor effectiveness under the NIR irradiation due to the synergy of combined chemo-phototherapy. As well, IONPs ability for the T2 weighted MRI contrast led to recognize cancer malignant lesions. Hence, such well-designed drug delivery system could be utilized for advanced multimodal theranostic approach.

Thermally sensitive polymers composed of compounds with light absorption ability are commonly applied in controllable pharmaceutical transport via irradiation of light triggers but most thermosensitive vehicles exhibit shortcomings as they are barely biocompatible and cannot be implanted [233]. Hence, microparticle composites of gold nanorods (AuNRs) and poly(N-vinylcaprolactam) (PVCL) were developed through stopped flow lithography which produced NIR light sensitive PVCL-AuNR microparticulates with uniform diameters allowing DOX encapsulation and local vaccination for NIR light sensitive cancer chemotherapy. PVCL-AuNR/DOX material demonstrated NIR light triggering DOX release via AuNRs photothermal influence that increased temperature locally to more than the PVCL-AuNR volume phase transition temperature. As well, PVCL-AuNR/DOX displayed controllable DOX release upon pulsed NIR radiation. Effective intracellular DOX release under NIR light radiation, cancer treatment and implantation capacities of PVCL-AuNR/DOX were approved.

Recently, light sensitive nanoarchitectures were developed using PLGA and a polyester that could encapsulate a photosensitizer drug and exhibit light stimulated medicine release [234]. The cytotoxicity of optimal nanocarriers was assessed in intracellular photosensitizer accumulation plus photodynamic therapy. It was found that such smart light responsive polyester had excellent light triggering features for cancer treatment.

The magnetic stimulation is one of the active stimuli, which is beneficial as a result of its simple usage and capacity to reach the carriers into the deeper organs. Magnetic responsive formulations can usually be incorporated by super-paramagnetic iron oxide Fe3O4 nanoparticles that are magnetized thru employing external magnetic fields [235]. The magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are added to hydrogels, loaded to the liposome center and functionalized with biocompatible polymers in order to obtain core-shell nanoscale architectures [236]. The MNPs cause local hyperthermia under magnetic stimulation which are employed in tumor treatments and promoting the drug release through enhanced diffusion, increased permeability of polymeric matrix or breakdown of the temperature labile linkers in drugs. The stimulation response level depends on the strength and frequency of magnetic field that results in a perfect drug delivery. The MNPs are utilized to trigger hyperthermia in clinical experiments of cancer treatments so that the stimulation is carried out for 1 h or longer and field strength up to 6 mT in pelvic area but 17 mT intra-cranially [237]. In most of the investigations, the MFH300F AC magnetic field (Mag-Force Nanotechnologies, GmbH, Berlin) is used which is sufficiently big that can accommodate human and provide magnetic fields in the range of 0–23 mT (at 100 kHz). The MNPs containing polymers are employed as stimuli responsive platforms in drug delivery using magnetic fields with high frequencies and different strengths from high (100 mT) to low (4 mT) values.

Recently, Fe3O4 superparamagnetic nanoparticles in addition to vancomycin antibiotic were incorporated to CS microbeads crosslinked by polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate with different lengths [237]. The biocompatibility, degradation and elution properties of the beads were examined along with their magnetic field stimulations using several field frequencies and strengths. A 30 min magnetic field stimulation caused up to 45% temporarily enhancement in daily elution which was dependent to the frequency, field strength and crosslinker length. The accelerated lysozyme degradation tests exhibited that the beads were degraded up to 70% after three days but they sustainably eluted antibiotic up to eight days. The in vitro cytotoxicity was not detected relative to the control indicating the clinical potential of the beads as the smart stimuli-controlled drug delivery vehicle.

The zein films containing magnetite NPs, zein/NPs, plus magnetite and acetaminophen, zein/NPs/drug, were prepared as the magnetic responsive formulations to obtain controlled release through diffusion and dissolution mechanisms [236]. The composite zein/NPs and zein/NPs/drug films were achieved through dispersing magnetite NPs in the solution containing zein and solution was casted in flat Teflon substrates. The drug was released from zein/NPs/drug during 2 h by applying magnetic fields. The diffusion mechanism was evaluated by means of an external magnet to stimulate the drug release using zein/NPs films placed in diffusion cells including acetaminophen for 24 h. The magnetite NPs existing within the films zein/NPs/drug and zein/NPs were durable and did not undergo adequately high oxidation levels to change their magnetic features. The dissolution and diffusion results exhibited the zein films efficiently behaved as stimulus responsive carriers which were stimulated with external magnetic fields. Thus, the drug release was adjusted through changing magnetic field strength.

Two stimuli were combined to fabricate magnetic field plus pH sensitive systems in order to enhance their therapeutic effects [238]. For this purpose, PEG modified γ-Fe2O3 composites (PEG/γ-Fe2O3) were achieved as the magnetic DOX carriers which displayed exceptional thermal properties applying alternating magnetic field, excellent magneto-thermal durability and great DOX loading efficiency. Additionally, DOX release experiments were carried out to study influences of alternating magnetic field and pH confirming DOX incorporated PEG/γ-Fe2O3 vehicles significantly respond into AMF as well as pH which caused considerably enhanced death of cancer cells compared to when only one stimulus was applied. As a result, such magnetic field and pH dual sensitive carriers could be in lung cancer therapy used with maximized therapeutic efficacy butminimized side effects.

Dual reduction and ultraviolet (UV) sensitive smart targeted carriers were utilized in chemotherapy [239]. The vehicles were functionalized by lactobionic acid to which Gd containing MRI contrast agent was added and GNSPL targeted or GNSPM non-targeted liposomes were developed indicating appropriate reduction and UV sensitive features, MRI activity plus biocompatibility. The drug incorporated liposomes, i.e. (D/GNSPM and D/GNSPL, exhibited stability for two weeks and highest drug accumulative release rates upon applying dual UV and reduction stimuli. In vitro treatment of cancer cells using D/GNSPL + UV considerably prevented cellular migration and growth. It was found that accumulation of D/GNSPL liposome vehicles was more efficient into tumor sites compared to D/GNSPM liposomal carriers, which was associated with the D/GNSPL targeting capacity. In vivo treatment using D/GNSPL + UV illustrated superior therapeutic efficiency without any side effects practically throughout the chemotherapy compared to pure DOX. Consequently, this MRI conducted targeted dual sensitive vehicle was a favorable therapeutic material in liver cancer treatment.

Enzymes have shown to be very imperative in diverse cellular metabolic activities with high specificity. Enzymes presenting at particular sites, tissues or organs with specific concentrations can be exploited to prepare enzyme responsive delivery systems [240]. The enzyme responsive systems are more effective and exhibit superior sensitivity and selectivity compared to other extra or intracellular stimuli responsive delivery systems. The extracellular matrix degradation in the diseased conditions can be triggered by particular enzymes, thus some extracellular systems are developed to deliver biomolecules like proteins and nucleic acids. The extracellular matrix based systems (elastin, fibrin and collagen) easily trigger the drug release by means of the proteases existing in the extracellular space to facilitate the payload delivery [241]. There are several drawbacks for enzyme responsive drug delivery systems including low carrier resistance to the enzymatic degradation as well as the optimal enzymatic activity usually happening under physiological conditions. Also, these systems reveal poor stability in a complex biological medium [241]. Accordingly, researchers investigate innovative enzyme responsive systems to overcome most of these limitations.

The tumor tissues typically have higher levels of specific enzymes; hence enzyme activity is utilized to stimulate the drug release. For example, cancer angiogenesis, proliferation and metastasis can cause overexpressing matrix metalloproteinases, MMPs, which are proteolytic enzymes with calcium and zinc dependent property [242]. The MMPs can digest some extracellular matrix components such as collagen, fibronectin, laminin, proteoglycans, vitronectin and elastin. Particularly, MMP9 and MMP2 have vital activities in metastasis, tumorigenesis, invasion and cell migration [243]. Certainly, nanocarriers which respond to MMPs can be prepared to deliver anticancer therapeutics. Usually, these nanocarriers can be functionalized by peptide sequence which is cleaved by MMPs. As an example, nanoparticles of a triblock copolymer, polylactide (PLLA)-peptide-PLLA, were synthesized and exhibited controlled in vitro drug release to respond the peptide cleaving by the MMP2 [244]. Nonetheless, because the MMP induced nanoparticles dissolution was happened within the extracellular matrix, the drugs released might demonstrate little intracellular uptakes once they were dissociated from their nanocarriers. Besides, when the drugs were released into the extracellular matrix, they might be aggregated or degraded within the milieu before contacting with cancer cells.

Recently, a nanocarrier was produced for controlled as well as targeted antitumor drug release plus real time bioimaging through combination of mesoporous silica NPs, MSN–SS–CDHA, and a dual enzyme/redox responsive disulfide linked carbon dot [245]. The carbon dot having the targeting and controlling capacities was obtained by polymerization of citric acid with HA (called CDHA). The MSN–SS–CDHA was more photostable with exceptionally biocompatible that could affect A549 cells with CD44 overexpressed receptors. Anticancer DOX drug was encapsulated to the mesoporous MSN and CDHA channels having 3 nm diameter that entirely limited the pore entering for the DOX loaded MSN having ~3 nm pore sizes to avoid the early DOX release and to enhance the anticancer efficacy till it was stimulated with a particular stimulus in the cancer milieu. The cytotoxicity and cell imaging tests established that the dual enzyme/redox sensitive DOX-incorporated MSN–SS–CDHA NPs delivered and controlled the DOX release to the cancer cells.

Fig. 34 exhibits the DOX release from nanocarriers by GSH and HAase treatment at pH 5.5 and 7.4. The viabilities of NIH-3T3 and A549 cells after 24 and 48 h treatment with the DOX-loaded MSN nanoparticles containing different DOX concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 35 . The fluorescence images ex vivo revealed that the MSN–SS–CDHA-DOX fluorescence at the tumor was very stronger compared to what was measured for the normal tissues which really facilitated DOX accumulation inside the targeted tissues. Fig. 36 shows the ex vivo imaging of lung, heart, liver, kidney, spleen and tumor after injection of DOX, MSN–SS–CDHA-DOX and MSN–SH–DOX to the tumor bearing mice. Nonetheless, the MSN–SH–DOX counterpart did not display or revealed much less drug accumulation and cytotoxicity for the tumor tissues. As well, the MSN–SS–CD was applied as the control to examine the targeting capability of MSN–SS–CDHA into the A549 cells. It was found that the MSNSS-CDHA had a greater A549 cell uptake by the endocytosis mediated with the CD44 receptors relative to the MSN–SS–CD, see Fig. 37 . Hence, these particular dual enzyme/redox responsive formulations were much valuable for various clinical applications to achieve bioimaging along with selective controlled and targeted therapeutic release.

Matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) enzyme sensitive and mannose receptor targeting gelatin nanocarriers, GNPs, were achieved and applied as receptor targeted and on demand controllable chemotherapy agents for lung cancer therapy [246]. MMPs are protease enzymes that are over-expressed within tumor cells during all cancer phases. As well, mannose receptors are over-expressed onto surface of cancer cells. Concanavalin A (conA) was used to modify the gelatin nanovehicles as it could bind to mannose existing onto surface glycoproteins of cancer cells and enhance specific nanocarrier uptake by cancer cells. Cisplatin drug was encapsulated into gelatin polymer nanocarriers (GNPs/CIS) and their stimuli responsive drug release characteristics to lung cancer cells were explored. These inhalable and smart cisplatin incorporated gelatin transport nanosystems were functionalized by conA and named GNPs/CIS/conA. Functionalization with conA enhanced mannose receptor specific GNPs/CIS/conA intracellular uptake by tumor cells and then great MMP amount within tumor cells released the CIS as a response indicating controllable CIS release was occurred. Upon 12 h incubation, the GNPs/CIS/conA showed enzyme triggering medicine release and suitable endocytosis than unmodified carriers. Also, the GNPs/CIS/conA anticancer performance against A549 lung cancer cells was considerably improved when containing MMP-2 enzyme which was associated with substantially boosted ROS production, stopped cell cycle in G2/M and S phases plus cancerous cells apoptosis. Consequently, inhalable GNPs/CIS/conA was introduced as the receptor targeted and on-demand nanovehicle that effectively delivered drug merely towards tumor sites.

A trypsin and ROS sensitive antitumor material was synthesized as a targeted carrier based on 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid functionalied gold nanoclusters (AuNCs/MUA) using EGFR (anti-epidermal growth factor receptor) affibody containing one 6-arginine tail [247]. Polyarginine group in affibody wrapped MTX (methotrexate) encapsulated AuNCs/MUA via electrostatic interactions and caused the rest targeted affibody moiety form specific bonds with overexpressed EGFR on tumor cells. The polyarginine chains on affibody formed a shell on affibody/MTX/AuNCs/MUA that were digested via trypsin and released the encapsulated MTX drug to accelerate AuNCs/MUA degradation by promoting ROS production. In fact, this affibody/MTX/AuNCs/MUA specific carrier which targeted EGFR-overexpressed tumor rapidly delivered satisfactory drug amounts into tumor cells, increased localized MTX plus ROS concentrations and finally eliminated by kidneys. Hence, such beneficial vehicle could be applied with superior treatment performance but little side effect for cancer therapy particularly for pancreatic cancer as it showed great trypsin levels.

Among different stimuli modes, the electrochemical redox stimulus is of particular attention due to its simple trigger mode and the importance of the potential variation in biological systems [248]. For instance, the redox potential is particularly attractive as the interior stimulus because glutathione (GSH) concentrations are significantly different in intracellular, 10 mM, and extracellular, 2 μM, media. Additionally, for most cancer cells, the lowest intracellular GSH level is four times greater compared to that of the normal cells [249]. Subsequent to employing the redox stimulus, gatekeepers permit release of drug except for the drug delivery formulation encounters a high GSH level.

In a recent work, a redox-responsive DDS was developed using heparosan plus deoxycholic acid, HSD, conjugates to efficiently treat laryngopharyngeal carcinoma [250]. These amphiphilic HSDs were self-assembled to nanoscale durable micelles within the aqueous solution with satisfactory DOX loading capacity, see Fig. 38 . The prepared micelles exhibited the GSH stimulated drug release that was almost 100% release of the DOX in presence of 10 mM GSH (high level). Fig. 39 reveals the drug release diagrams for the DOX-loaded HD and HSD2 micelles with and without using the GSH. Also, HSD micelles were internalized FaDu carcinoma cells through endocytosis mediated by clathrin that was energy dependent, rapid and effectual. The DOX@HSD promoted growth inhibition to the FaDu malignant cells and revealed at least 10 times greater selectivity compared to that measured for the normal COS-7 cells (Fig. 40 ). Thus, the redox responsive systems with appropriate biocompatibility were favorable for the clinical laryngopharyngeal cancer therapy.

A nanocarrier was achieved using redox responsive nanoparticles of P[(2-((2-((camptothecin)-oxy)ethyl)disulfanyl)ethylmethacrylate)-co-(2-(d-galactose)methylmethacryl-ate)], P(MACPTS-co-MAGP), and AgNPs to be used in delivery of the anticancer camptothecin, CPT, drug and investigating drug release via the CPT fluorescence recovery [251]. The CPT was bound to side chains of the polymer through a disulfide bond that was redox responsive and attached over the surface of AgNPs and caused the CPT fluorescence quenching (off state) by surface energy transfer influence of the nanoparticle. When exposed to the GSH, CPT release was occurred as the disulfide bond was broken which was resulted in the CPT fluorescence recovery (on state). Thus, such system could be applied to carry out the real time CPT delivery and release.

Several hybrids of polypyrrole (PPy) and montmorillonite (Mt) were fabricated when pyrrole monomer was electropolymerized in situ in presence of dispersed Mt using aspirin drug molecules so that the aspirin was loaded in Mt-PPy hybrid [252]. The Mt-PPy hybrid had benefits of both Mt with rather high surface area as well as PPy electroresponsive characteristic; thus, it was used as the electrically adjustable release system. The electro-stimulated release of drug from Mt-PPy hybrid was considerably increased compared to the pure PPy signifying the electro-responsive hybrids could be potential candidates for smart drug release applications.

Recently, a redox and pH dual responsive copper/glyglycine/prednisolone succinate incorporated nanoliposomal, NL, sludge was prepared [253]. It was found that the relative ratio of phospholipids much affected NL size thus altered the drug loading degree. The in vitro test established that the optimal release kinetics for the NL sludge was close to the permeation ex vivo tests which demonstrated efficient prednisone succinate, PS, transdermal delivery into pig skin with high resemblance to the anatomy of human skin. The redox and pH stimuli responsive activity by the NL sludge revealed improved features in vivo on Sprague-Dawley rat. Also, the NL sludge exhibited the utmost PS release in 24 h which was satisfactory and in the therapeutic efficacy range for the PS dosage. The in vivo experiments illustrated the highest PS absorption once inflammatory conditions are induced; therefore, exceptional redox and pH responsive characteristics by the NL sludge are approved. Hence, the copper/glyglycine/prednisolone succinate loaded NL sludge had a substantial capacity in treatment of chronic inflammatory diseases like tumor necrosis factor receptor related periodic syndrome; besides, it could release effective doses of corticosteroid and act as an ideal transdermal vehicle with powerful pharmaceutical effectiveness.

Dual redox and pH responsive medicine carrier was developed using a star shaped folate functionalied PEG copolymer and biodegrading castor oil to tackle some difficulties related to DOX application including little dissolution, side effects and great cytotoxicity [254]. Redox responsive disulfide bond and pH responsive imine bond were introduced onto the copolymer chains that formed stable micelles trough self-assembly indicating 91.67 nm diameter, fine size distribution as well as 2.7 mg/L critical micelle concentration. Micelles disassembly was assessed through their morphology variations happened upon endolysosomal exposure at pH = 5.5 and large GSH concentrations. DOX drug was loaded into the hydrophobic core of micelles which displayed 96.13 and 15.93% load efficiency and load capacity, respectively. The DOX release in vitro from the micellar carriers was redox and pH dually stimulated. Also, the copolymer did not illustrate any in vitro toxicity to HDF cells whereas the DOX containing micelles had boosted toxicity to HeLa cells. Therefote, the redox and pH responsive smart micelles were favorable DOX nanocarriers which acted well as anticancer agents.

Replacing the insulin therapy is essential to manage diabetes type 1 that is recognized with total insulin deficit [255]. The optimum glycaemic regulation, which can be evaluated with glycated haemoglobin as well as avoiding hypo/hyperglycaemic excursions, prevent diabetes associated complications. Using the insulin pump has been noticeably increased in the last decades having useful influences on controlling the glycaemic, therapy pleasure and life quality. The emergence and development of glucose sensors technique leads to continuously glucose measurements by detecting real time glucose amounts which is related to the insulin treatment. Currently, predictive low glucose as well as low glucose suspending systems have been applied in clinical practices in order to alleviate the hypoglycaemia and to offer the primary stage for the glucose regulation. An advanced method is using closed-loop systems or artificial pancreas which slowly enhances and declines the insulin release by a glucose responsive process to reduce the hypo and hyperglycaemic, respectively. Random clinical tests during recent years have established the possibility, safety and effectiveness of the method. Moreover, the first closed-loop system for a single hormone recent was approved by the FDA that verifies this is a successful therapy for type 1 diabetes patients.

It is known that daily subcutaneous multi dosage insulin injections are conventionally requisite to treat type 1 as well as advanced type 2 diabetes but such therapy can unfortunately result in insufficient glycemic control together with pain [256]. The glucose responsive formulations that are prepared based on glucose oxidase, glucose binding protein, phenylboronic acid and concanavalin A (Con A) sense the glucose level in blood and release insulin in consistent with the glucose concentration variation confirming they are extremely advantageous in closed-loop delivery of insulin. Among these platforms, the Con A-sugar affinity systems have a high promise in clinical application because of its highest glucose specificity. The first in vivo assay was accomplished in a domestic diabetic live pig by means of an artificial implantable pancreas prepared using dextran-Con A crosslinked hydrogel [257]. Similarly, some glucose responsive microgels and hydrogels were fabricated by glycidyl methacrylate functionalized dextran, (Dex-GMA)-Con A. Particularly, microgel was a very promising formulation due to it can be employed in subcutaneous and intramuscular injections [258].

It is known that individual patients exhibit different disease expressions, co-morbidities, symptoms, genetic susceptibility and drugs sensitivity. Thus, personal treatment denoting modification of medical diabetes care for each patient is of great attention. Also, enough insulin must be delivered for each patient consistent with diverse degrees of insulin resistance to tackle the low insulin efficacy and to preserve normally the glucose tolerance. This individual insulin treatment may cause tight glucose regulation compared to conventional therapy, thus reversely increase insulin sensitivity by the patients [259]. Therefore, for an effective glucose responsive delivery of insulin, it is preferred to attain controlled real time insulin release once the insulin containing nano- and microcarriers are injected to the patients.

The insulin injection is known as an effective treatment method for most patients with the type I diabetes and several with type II diabetes. Recently, a smart drug carrier was prepared which released insulin by variation of the glucose level in blood (Fig. 41 ) [260]. The (5-ethyl-2-phenyl-1,3,2-dioxaborinan-5-yl) methyl 2-bromoacetate, EPDMB, was combined by β-CD through bromination to achieve the β-CD-EPDME as the glucose-responsive insulin delivery system. The drug carrier contains cyclodextrin which encapsulated phenylboronate and insulin that showed sensitivity to cis-diols existing in several saccharides. Furthermore, the β-CD-EPDME loaded the insulin and released insulin nearly entirely in glucose presence. The glucose triggered isolated phenylboronic acid attacked β-CD cavity to form a host-guest complex that released the insulin encapsulated in the cavity. Also, the secondary structure of released insulin from the complex β-CD-EPDME@insulin was preserved. Also, the carrier exhibited low cytotoxicity confirming this benign glucose responsive platform could be applied for the diabetes treatments.

Development of oral delivery systems for insulin therapeutics is very significant. In this context, a glucose-responsive nanocarrier was fabricated for the insulin loading [261]. The nanocarrier displayed low cytotoxicity toward Caco-2 cells and outstanding durability in protein solutions. The in vitro insulin release was assessed by pH and glucose triggers. To increase the insulin oral bioavailability, the insulin containing glucose responsive nanocarrier was incorporated in a hyaluronic acid hydrogel to overcome many barriers and afford protection of insulin throughout its delivery. The hypoglycemic influence in the insulin oral delivery was evaluated in vivo. The oral administration of insulin containing glucose responsive hydrogels comprising nanocarriers to the diabetic rats revealed that the released insulin led to efficient hypoglycemic influence in extended time than the native insulin loaded nanocarrier.

The glucose responsive ability and blood stability are both vital for the drug carriers used for insulin controlled release. Also, it is important to decrease the macrophage response by the carriers and self-assembled zwitterionic micelles that alleviated recognition with immune cells can solve this drawback. Recently, zwitterionic glucose-responsive dialdehyde starch-based micelles were developed using sulfobetaine, SB, hydrophilic shell and 3-aminophenylboronic acid, APBA, as a glucose responsive group to dialdehyde starch, DAS, backbones, SB-DAS-APBA [262]. The insulin release by the nanocarriers had sensitivity onto the glucose amount and it was rapid at pH 7.4 when glucose concentration reached 3 mg/mL. It was established that the zwitterionic micelles had little cytotoxicity and cytobiocompatibility against A549 cells. Moreover, the PEGylated micelles were prepared using hydrophilic shell of mPEG monomethyl ether and the glucose responsive APBA group onto the DAS backbone, mPEG-DAS-APBA. The confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry data established that the PEGylated and zwitterion coating micelles had diverse recognition processes when they contacted with macrophages (Fig. 42 ).

The DOX fluorescence revealed that after 4 h, most of the DOX containing mPEG-DAS-APBA micelles were positioned into nuclei but the SB-DAS-APBA micelles were somewhat distinguished in both cytosol and nuclei. Besides, the fluorescence peaks of mPEG-DAS-APBA micelles within macrophage cells were superior to those of the SB-DAS-APBA micelles. Thus, the self-assembled zwitterionic SB-DAS-APBA carrier micelles were glucose-responsive for the insulin delivery and simultaneously decreased the macrophage response activity.

Microneedle carriers indicating self-regulation cargo release were developed for improved diabetes therapy [263]. For this purpose, a hydrogel microneedle patch was fabricated using phenylboronic acid demonstrating exceptional biocompatibility and glucose sensitive medication transport characteristic at 37 °C. The glucose sensitivity of hydrogel microneedle patch was related to existence of reversible phenylborate ester crosslinks occurred using phenylboronic acid and diol moieties. Furthermore, these reversible crosslinks improved the mechanical strength of patch in skin attachment confirming it was a smart, non-toxic and durable transdermal vehicle.

Microwaves are nonionizing electromagnetic waves having frequencies in the range of 300 MHz and 300 GHz [264]. Microwave is not a heat form whereas it is an energy form that can interact thermally or non-thermally with different substances. The microwave technology can medically be employed as a part of cancer therapy. Current medical developments apply microwaves in hyperthermia ablation to treat intrahepatic primary cholangiocarcinoma, renal cancer, metastatic liver cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, hepatic colorectal metastases, benign thyroid nodules, lung cancer and dysfunctional uterine bleeding. Microwaves can cause various biological influences depending on the field frequency, strength, wave form and exposure duration that can mainly be related to the microwave heating. Nevertheless, it was proposed that some non-thermal microwave effects can bring about several kinds of molecular variations and transformations [265].

Microwave radiation is able to improve the skin penetration. Also, it can increase the transdermal drug permeation with no substantial skin temperature elevation. The microwaves lead to drug permeation by creation of space in lipid domains of stratum corneum to structureless parts which is beneficial for the cancer therapy [265]. It is known that melanoma leads to ~80% of global skin cancer-related deaths [266]. It is produced by melanocyte cells that make pigments and placed in the basal deep layer of skin called epidermis. Lots of anticancer drugs are hydrophilic having small oil:water partition coefficients. Thus, their efficacy in the melanoma treatment is decreased due to insufficient penetration depth to the layers of peripheral skin named dermis on which tumors are generally situated.

Recently, microwaves were applied as the penetration enhancer to deliver anticancer 5-fluorouracil drug using submicron particles of folate-chitosan into the melanoma and skin cells both in vitro and in vivo [266]. The submicron conjugated particles of chitosan/carboxymethyl/5-fluorouracil/folate were produced and their pharmacokinetics, retention and permeation into skin, in vitro cytotoxicity to SKMEL-28 melanoma cells as well as intracellular transferring were evaluated in melanoma/skin cell therapy using microwave irradiation in 5 + 5 min at 2450 MHz. It was found that the particle/drug retention in vivo and in vitro was improved in the skin treated with the microwave. It was assisted through the chitosan conjugated drug and microwave which fluidized the lipid and protein areas of dermis and epidermis. The folate-chitosan particles uptake via melanoma was facilitated by lipid rafting path. It was stimulated through microwave fluidizing lipids and proteins in cell membrane to increase the cytotoxicity of drug. The pharmacokinetic test in vivo designated that skin therapy using microwave improved drug preservation while did not affect its permeation. Hence, the synergistic influence of submicron particles and microwaves enhanced the intracellular drug delivery and skin drug retention.

In another effort, core–shell mesoporous Fe3O4@mZnO NPs were synthesized which exhibited microwave to heat and magnetic responsive characteristics as well as large specific surface area, 93 m2 g−1 [267]. Also, the nanoparticles revealed great pore size, 6.7 nm, which was suitable in drug adsorption physically. Robust exceptional microwave to heat and magnetic responsive features for the Fe3O4@mZnO NPs could be used to develop nanoparticles with potential application in drug targeting as well as combined chemotherapy and microwave-thermal therapy.

Several hollow mesoporous cobalt ferrite NPs, H-mCoxFe3-xO4, were synthesized so that the monodispersed H-mCoxFe3-xO4 exhibited an average diameter of 280 nm, 58.03 m2 g−1 surface area, 7.21 nm pore size and 0.13 cm3 g−1 pore volume which were appropriate for drug loading [269].
