Also, high magnetization saturation (42.2 emu/g) was measured for the H-mCoxFe3-xO4 which was useful in targeted drug delivery and the temperature of sodium chloride mixture was raised from ambient temperature to 50 °C within 180 s. Under microwave radiation, the drug release rate was more rapid compared to that measured with stirring and nearly 64% of VP16 release was happened subsequent to 6 on/off microwave pulses confirming the H-mCoxFe3-xO4 NPs were promising drug carriers for application in microwave-triggered controlled drug release.

Ultrasounds are mechanical longitudinal waves which can be propagated in a medium by pressure variation with greater frequencies compared to the audible ones for the human (20 Hz and 20 kHz) [270]. Ultrasound waves are propagated with frequencies greater than 20 kHz. The ultrasound technique is well embedded in the medical fields because it is inexpensive and benign. Furthermore, the system can be moved to the patient instead of placing patients into the system (such as MRI and CT scanners). The ultrasound irradiation is employed for both diagnosis (imaging) and therapeutic (tumor tissue ablation) applications. Ultrasound is controlled through changing diverse parameters including intensity and frequency that are very important. Frequency can modulate the penetration depth and potential cavitation and intensity varies the energy amount delivered to the favorite position [271]. As well, the exposure period and the pulse sequence called duty cycle could be altered. Some biological influences are made by ultrasound irradiation, which are useful for drug delivery and treatments. Some of the most significant effects are thermal/mechanical effects and radiation forces.

Several injectable and ultrasound (US)-responsive nanoparticle aggregates (NPAs) were fabricated which were disintegrated to nanoscale, slow release DDSs and could be targeted to desired locations through applying local low energy US [271]. Different from the microbubble drug carriers suffering from instability, the properties of mechanically activated NPAs that contained polymeric nanoparticles could be changed through regulating the polymer molecular weight, the size of nanoparticles precursors and the quantity of excipients us to prepare the NPAs. The nanoparticles were loaded by DOX drug and utilized for the treatment of tumors using ultrasound irradiation. The in vivo experiments using mouse models confirmed considerably improved efficacy of tumor targeting by the US-activated NPAs than those of the intact NPAs and PLGA as control nanoparticles (with and without US irradiation). It was found that once the DOX-incorporated NPAs were injected and exposed to local US irradiation, the nanoparticles were more concentrated at the tumor site which caused a substantially decrease in tumor size relative to the tumor treated using a 20-fold greater dose of the pure DOX drug.

The US-responsive nanodroplets are smart formulations that offer image guided treatment for several illnesses and particularly sarcomas [272]. Some smart multifunctional curcumin-incorporated perfluorohexane-chitosan nanodroplets were prepared and used in drug transport and US contrast imaging. The optimum formulation revealed 101.2 nm diameter and drug encapsulation of 77.8%. Application of US with 1 MHz (2 W/cm2) frequency in 4 min stimulated 63.5% curcumin release from the optimum formulation. It was recognized that the perflourohexane concentration and the amount of acoustic droplet vaporization had significant effects in US-activated drug release. The B-mode US-imaging established robust US contrast by the chitosan nanodroplets yet at low amounts through the droplet change to bubble. The US sensirive nanodroplets cytotoxicity was assessed by US irradiation in-vitro against 4T1 human breast carcinoma cells. The cell growing inhibition by the curcumin containing nanodroplets was considerably enhanced under US irradiation. Hence, such US-responsive curcumin-containing chitosan/perfluorohexane nanodroplets were valuable in imaged guided cancer treatment.

A local DDS was achieved using superhydrophobic titanium oxide nanotube (S-TNT) arrays and US-controlled release trigger [273]. The hydrophilic TNT arrays were changed to superhydrophobic ones by treatment with 1H,1H,2H, 2H-perfluorooctyl-triethoxysilane. The S-TNT arrays were served as drug carriers which did not necessitate additional sealing reaction because they had outstanding isolation influence by the entrapped air layer on their surfaces. Diverse drug amounts were loaded to the S-TNTs arrays through controlling the structures of arrays (diameters and lengths of tubes) and the initial drug concentration. In order to afford a stimulus-responsive carrier, US irradiation was applied as an effective drug release stimulus. The trapped air layer could selectively be detached via US exposure to release the loaded drug in a multiple controlled way. All drugs that are soluble in nonpolar solvents were suitable for such local drug delivery approach.

ZnFe2O4@mZnO (mesoporous ZnO)/oxygen rich (composed of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups) porous graphitic carbon nitride (oxy-p-g-C3N4) vehicles were prepared as core@shell ZnFe2O4@mZnO/oxy-p-g-C3N4 magnetic architectures indicating ultrasound and pH dual responsive characteristics in targeting curcumin (Cur) transport [274]. Concentrated ultrasound was applied as efficient stimulus on tumor cells to deliver echogenic medicine and decrease unwanter effects by chemotherapy. Ultrasound and pH triggers were applied as external physical and internal chemical stimuli to achieve targeting Cur release into specific tumor sites. The magnetic ZnFe2O4@mZnO-Oxy-p-g-C3N4 and ZnFe2O4@mZnO nanomaterials exhibited 67.37 and 51.39% loading efficiencies, respectively, and water high stability during 24 h. Amounts of Cur release in 72 h were greater at pH = 5.5 from both ZnFe2O4@mZnO-Oxy-p-g-C3N4 and ZnFe2O4@mZnO (56.05 and 70.28%, respectively) compared to those measured at pH = 7.4 (45.40 and 66.26%, respectively).

When the interactions between drug carriers and therapeutics are of electrostatic nature, incorporation of competitive ligands could affect the drug release [275]. As an instance, PEG-PGlu and PEG-PAsp were interacted electrostatically by cisplatin and formed micelles by self-assembly so that the polymer carboxyl group was interacted with the cisplatin amino group. The drug release was not occurred under normal conditions but more than 50% cisplatin release was happened by adding chloride ions into the PBS indicating it was an ion sensitive system [276]. Irinotecan containing PEG-b-PAA micelles were obtained as ionic complexes which indicated at pH 7.4, the micelles displayed low release but considerably increased release was measured in acidic solutions [277]. The cationic MTX and DOX drugs were interacted with the negatively charged PAA and it was recognized that the interactions occurred between the cationic drug and the polyionic copolymer had noticeably affected biological activity of the system [278].

Drug conjugates are formed upon conjugation process which is covalently binding of drugs and/or prodrugs onto numerous synthetic or natural carriers that will be applied in specific areas. Diverse kinds of carriers can be employed such as antibodies, polymers, proteins, peptides, carbohydrates, enzymes and lipids. Conjugation is known as a very significant approach for targeted and controlled pharmaceuticals delivery/release. So far, different conjugations have been produced including antibody-drug, polymer–drug, peptide–drug, protein–drug, carbohydrate–drug, enzyme–drug, lipid–drug and T-cell dual affinity re-targeting conjugations. Drug conjugates could enhance and modify pharmacokinetic, pharmacodynamics, toxicity and stability of drugs.

Antibody–drug conjugation called ADC has been established as an effective method for targeted drug transport in which the payloads/drugs (generally cellular toxins) are usually bound onto monoclonal antibody carriers through chemical linkers []. The monoclonal antibodies provide targeted drug delivery towards the desired tissues like tumor cells. It is expected that the ADC is stable within the blood fluid while releasing its payload into targeted tissues through pH variations or enzymatic break down of bonds. The drug in the conjugate can reach specific sites and hence minimizes releasing to non-targeted organs. Such high selectivity perfectly decreases adverse effects of usual chemotherapy due to less chemotoxin amounts are needed in selective targeted drug delivery. Furthermore, the proximity of bioactive/cytotoxic medicines towards linkers and antibody might efficiently decrease the toxicity. Also, the conjugate may not reach organs other than targeted cells into which the drug should be released which can be because of its steric hindrance. This elegant approach is interesting and explored by scientists and pharmaceutical organizations.

If chemotherapy agents form random conjugates with monoclonal antibody materials, heterogeneous ADCs are developed which exhibit non-optimized pharmaceutical features. Homogeneous ADCs have been simply generated based on harnessing human CD38 catalytic domain plus its dinucleotide derivative covalent inhibitor called ADP-ribosyl cyclase supported ADCs, i.e. ARC-ADCs [279]. Such technique was advanced via ARC-ADCs design and synthesis by changing drug/antibody ratio. By altering location and number of CD38 fused into the antibody directing human C-type lectin-like molecule-1 (hCLL-1), ARC-ADCs of drug/antibody ratios of 4 and 2 were quickly obtained and loaded with toxic monomethyl auristatin F (MMAF). Contrary to anti-hCLL-1 ARC-ADC carrying two drugs, anti-hCLL-1 ARC-ADC with drug/antibody ratio = 4 showed extremely high anticancer capacity as it killed in vivo and in vitro hCLL-1-positive acute myeloid leukemia cells. Consequently, the ARC-ADC was a desirable candidate against acute myeloid leukemia.

It is known that site-specific payload attachment onto the antibody using adjustable drug/antibody ratio are extremely appropriate to generate homogeneous ADCs [280]. In this context, N-phenyl-divinylsulfonamide was utilized as the linker to achieve site-specific homogeneous ADCs of drug/antibody ratio = 4 using disulfide re-bridging method. Synthesis of some MMAE (monomethyl auristatin E) linkers was performed and drug-linker materials containing electron donating fragments onto the linker phenyl groups were highly stable. Conjugates of MMAE linker with herceptin and cetuximab were synthesized which were Her2 and EGFR targeted materials, respectively. These conjugates were highly effective and selective in killing in vitro target-positive cancerous cells. Also, the EGFR targeting conjugates displayed substantial in vivo antitumor capacities.

Polymer-drug conjugates are generally formed through attachment of drug into biocompatible and water soluble polymers. Polymers may improve drug pharmacokinetic behavior and solubility, prevent drug degradation by enzymes and efflux with P-glycoprotein, as well as stimulate targeted drug release upon pH variations and/or existence of enzymes [281]. Advanced polymer conjugates contain a target-pursuing function/group like antibody ligands/light chains which can be used in targeted pharmaceutical release. Notably, high doses of some polymer conjugates like dextran and PEG polymers could be toxic to the kidney, liver, and/or other animal/human organs.

Stimulus sensitive polymer-drug conjugate materials are auspicious chemotherapy agents. Recently, polymer-drug conjugates were developed based on very active antimitotic monomethyl auristatin E bound onto dendritic polyglycerolsulfate and polyglycerol by a self-immolative and reduction cleaving disulfide linkage [282]. HeLa cervix carcinoma and A549 human lung carcinoma cells were utilized to evaluate viability tests which exhibited the drug toxicity was decreased when it was conjugated onto polymeric materials so that the sulfate containing conjugate illustrated higher activity compared to the sulfate free conjugates. Sustained drug release was measured from the polymers and greatly delayed toxic activity was seen upon conjugation by sulfate free polymer because it showed lower cell uptake. Hence, reduction cleaving conjugates displayed desirable in vitro effects and appropriate in vivo toleration.

In another study, polymer-DOX conjugates were achieved using hydrophilic copolymer plus a DOX containing hydrophobic chain [283]. DOX was utilized as the hydrophobic chemotherapy medication served as the hydrophobic hanging group which induced formation of polymer-DOX conjugate via self-assembly. Self-assembled polymer-drug conjugate was obtained and encapsulated with pure DOX to achieve drug incorporated polyprodrug micelles of small critical micelle concentration that was cost-effective in view of using low concentration of costly DOX medicine. Amidase was applied to facilitate DOX cleavage from polymer-DOX conjugates through breakdown of DOX incorporated polyprodrug micelles which triggered high DOX release within somewhat acidic solutions. The DOX-containing polyprodrug micelles illustrated greater in vitro death to sarcoma cells. Accordingly, such polymer-DOX conjugates could be effectively employed for chemotherapy.

Carbohydrate-drug conjugates are fabricated and used to increase effectiveness of chemotherapy drugs to become specific carriers against cancer cells. To improve treatments of bowel inflammation illnesses, colon-specific carrier for were achieved through conjugation of 5-aminosalicylic acid (ASAL) onto chitosan (CS) using 4-aminobenzoyl linker [284]. Synthesis of ASAL-CS azoconjugate materials was done using N-(4-aminobenzoyl)-CS and salicylic acid (yields = 55–66%). ASAL-CS conjugates revealed high stability as ASAL release was not happened at 37 °C during 24 h into simulated colon phosphate buffer, intestinal phosphate buffer and gastric HCl solutions with pH values of 7.4, 6.8 and 1.2, respectively. Amounts of ASAL release from ASAL-CS conjugate within these three fluids including rat gastrointestinal content were evaluated and compared to ASAL release from sulfasalazine employing azoreductable sulfapyridine vehicle. Nearly 70% ASAL release was measured from sulfasalazine during 24 h within colon fluid including rat colon contents but about 25% drug release was detected for ASAL-CS conjugate in 24 h. The targeted colon specific capacity of ASAL-CS azoconjugate was also approved but it was suggested that the effectiveness of this material would be enhanced through preparation of its nanostructures of higher specific surface areas plus exposure of azo bonds with bacterial enzyme.

In another effort, conjugate of O-carboxymethyl CS hydrogel and caffeic acid as well as its polyacrylamide composite were developed by electron beam radiation [285]. Loading of doxycycline into these hydrogels was done and the drug release was explored within several solutions. Effects of electron beam radiation intensity and polyacrylamide concentration onto hydrogels features were evaluated, i.e. drug load/release, swelling, gel amount and mechanical characteristics. The doxycycline release form hydrogels within several fluids indicated non-Fickian diffusion mechanism which was excellently matched Korsmeyer-Peppas and Higuchi models. A greater amount of in vitro doxycycline release was detected from composite hydrogel compared to other sample. These dressing hydrogels did not demonstrate any cytotoxic effects. Furthermore, doxycycline containing hydrogels illustrated resistant bacterial growth inhibitions towards Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. Consequently, such hydrogels were suitable dressing materials for bactericidal wound healing purposes.

It is well recognized that combination of functional proteins and medicines in one unit donates extra synergistic benefits. Nevertheless, various cargos reveal totally diverse physicochemical features; hence, co-delivery of therapeutics by systemic administration has always been a great challenge. Recently, a protein-drug conjugate was developed based on human serum albumin/Pt(IV)-dibenzocyclooctyne/chlorine6-catalase, called HSA-DBC-C6AT, through modifying C6AT and cisplatin encapsulated HSA by pH responsive 3-(azidomethyl)-4-methyl-2,5-furandione linker that was subsequently assembled by DC via click chemistry [286]. The dynamical covalent bonds linked protein to linker bridged protein and drug which triggered minor break down of bonds and drug/protein release as totally bioactive agents within acidic tumor environment. Therefore, multifunctions of HSA-DBC-C6AT provided effective in vitro cytosolic transducing influence, exceptional blood half-lives during systemic circulation, and substantial antitumor capacity through combined chemotherapy by cisplatin plus photodynamic therapy boosted with catalase assisted influence of hypoxia at the tumor site.

Recently, conjugates of accurately introduced medicines and adjustable pharmacokinetic features were developed using Designed Ankyrin Repeat Proteins, called DARPins, attached onto polypeptides with diverse lengths in order to create proteins of preferred half-lives and find the highest effectiveness [287]. EpCAM targeted DARPin-monomethyl auristatin F conjugate, attached onto polypeptides with various lengths plus controls materials of non-binding DARPin were prepared to achieve enhanced permeability and retention effect which indicated several minutes to 20.6 h half-lives in mice. The conjugates all established great specific performance and toxicity to human tumor cells as they indicated small nM IC50 values although polypeptides lengths and types were changed. Influence of all PASylated conjugates was examined on nude mice with HT29 tumor xenografts and it was shown that despite their various sizes, these conjugates were exceptionally stable under repeating systemic injection of 300 nmol/kg. Also, the conjugates of middle half-life and size displayed the highest antitumor activities and this result was related to compromised effects of penetration into the tumor tissue plus serum half-life.

In another investigation, a DOX-protein conjugate was achieved using PEGylated keratin attached to bioreducible disulfide linker and acid cleaving hydrazone group [288]. DOX release from the nanoconjugate was controllable as well as reduction and pH dual sensitive accompanied by a small 5.5% early drug leak into the simulated physiological fluid. This DOX-protein conjugate illustrated in vitro DOX transport to tumor cells nuclei and improved antitumor efficiency confirming it was a favorable material of minimum toxicity for tumor therapy.

Design and synthesis of peptide-drug conjugates is known as an effective technology to prevent primary tumor progress, cancer metastasis and patient decease. Recently, E-selectin-binding peptide was used as the metastasis inhibitor plus targeting agent that was conjugated to PEG and SN38 drug to achieve PEGylated amphiphilic peptide-drug conjugate, called SN38-Pep-PEG [289]. This amphiphilic conjugate formed nanostructures through self-assembly which indicated active targeting towards tumor vasculatures via peptide and passive targeting into tumor cells through the enhanced permeability and retention effect. The multifunctional SN38-Pep-PEG nanoformulation conjugate prodrug diminished tumor progress like the SN38. Furthermore, the conjugate prolonged the lifetime of mice with primary HCT116 tumor but pure SN38 and clinically used irinotecan prodrug medicines did not exhibit this effect. Also, it inhibited colony formation by B16–F10 within mice lung. Hence, such peptide-drug nanoformulation prodrug conjugate was a promising tumor cells targeting which could be applied in chemotherapy.

In another work, pro-apoptotic and cancer-specific drug-drug conjugate nanostructure was developed through conjugation of cathepsin B-cleaving peptide (FRRG=Phe-Arg-Arg-Gly) and pro-apoptotic peptide drug (SMAC, AVPIAQ = Ala-Val-Pro-Ile-Ala-Gln) onto DOX molecules to produce DOX-FRRG-SMAC which formed nanostructures by self-assembly [290]. The conjugate was employed in treatment of drug-resistant cancers which improved chemotherapy efficiency. It exhibited specific cleavage to toxic DOX and pro-apoptotic SMAC merely within sarcoma cells overexpressed cathepsin B which caused synergism between chemotherapy and pro-apoptotic effect. Furthermore, the conjugate was employed in intravenous injection to mice with MCF-7 breast tumor and illustrated extremely targeting accumulation with the tumor tissue through enhanced permeability and retention effect which released DOX and SMAC and established chemotherapy and pro-apoptotic synergism. Additionally, DOX-FRRG-SMAC conjugate significantly prevented tumor progress and totally enhanced mice survival with metastatic lung cancer. Hence, the cancer-specific nanostructured DOX-FRRG-SMAC conjugate could be successfully employed in treatment of drug resilient sarcomata.

Lipid-drug conjugates (LDCs) are effectively utilized in treatment of various diseases with higher performance but lower side effects than pure drugs. In a recent investigation, nicotine was attained using tobacco leaves and applied to stimulate smoking termination and reducing hazards of lung cancer and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease [291]. It is known that encapsulation of an active ingredient such as nicotine within lipid nanoarchitectures can effectively diminish the irritation effect but nicotine is a hydrophilic material and its incorporation into lipid nanomaterials with great cargo loading is challenging. Thus, conjugates of drug-lipid were developed using nicotine and diverse fatty acids so that lipid-nicotine nanostructures composed of sufficient drug molecules were achieved. The nicotine entrapment efficacy was ~50% that was 4 folds greater than non-conjugated formulation by ~10% because of improved nicotine lipophilicity via boosted electrostatic interactions among nicotine and fatty acids including positive and negative charges, respectively. All materials revealed submicron mean particle sizes in the range of 150–350 nm. Thus, lipid-nicotine conjugate nanoarchitectures of large loading efficacy produced using hydrogenated sunflower oil (lipid), Kolliwax® S and stearic acid (counter ion) could be used in prevention of lung cancer.

Lipid-drug conjugates were prepared as efficient nanomaterials to orally deliver pemetrexed diacid and evaluate their in vivo, ex vivo and in vitro characteristics [292]. For this purpose, pemetrexed diacid salt was formed using stearic acid that was followed via cold homogenization to achieve LDCs nanoformulations. The lipid and drug conjugation was approved by amide bond formation within the LDCs which exhibited spherical shape and nanoparticle size of 121.9 ± 1.85 nm with −51.6 ± 1.23 mV zeta potential plus 81.0 ± 0.89% capsulation efficacy. The LDCs gut permeation ex vivo displayed greatly enhanced drug penetration than the pure drug confirming substantial cytotoxicity after 48 and 24 h. Additionally, area under the plasma concentration-time curve after 24 h for the pemetrexed diacid from in optimum LDC nanoformulation was 4.22 times superior to that of orally administered pemetrexed diacid fluid. Consequently, LDC could be applied in pemetrexed diacid oral transport for anticancer chemotherapy.

Galantamine, Rivastigmine and Donepezil are acetyl cholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors which are employed as pharmaceuticals in treatment of mental signs of Alzheimer syndrome [293]. These advantageous ativities are associated with the capacity of these inhibitors in elevating endogenous acetylcholine (ACh) in M1 muscarinic brain receptor. Nevertheless, they exhibit side effects like dizziness, vomiting, nausea, insomnia, changed heart rate and appetite loss due to non-specifically M2-M5 muscarinic sub-types activation within several organs. Consequently, selective development of M1 receptor agonists could be rationally carried out but this is a challenge because orthosteric sites of receptor subtypes are greatly homologs. On contrary, the allosteric sites are specific which hence permit specific targeting based on positive allosteric modulators (PAMs). PAMs in M1 receptors lack agonist capacity but upon binding they increase orthosteric ACh ligand bonding affinity. The most significant PAMs drawback is that they indicate low brain bioavailability. Accordingly, surface functionalized LDCs nanostructures were provided using PAMs of M1 receptors which improved their brain bioavailability. Upon co-administration by AChE inhibitors, these LDCs illustrated increased their effectiveness while reduced therapeutic dosage plus side effects.

Nowadays, nanomaterial-drug conjugates are widely investigated as interesting nanobiomaterials in fabrication high performance drug vehicles [294]. In this context, carbon dots (CDs) are attractive materials for tumor theranostics however they are rapidly cleared by renal system because they are highly hydrophilic and small. This problem was solved by synthesis of covalent conjugates based on pH sensitive hydrophobic CDs and DOX drug using acid-cleavable hydrazine (Hyd) bridge linker. The DOX-Hyd-CDs illustrated 48.23% DOX amount. After that, self-assembly of the pH responsive CDs-Hyd-DOX prodrug with a PEG-based surfactant (i.e., hyperbranched degradable PEG-PO-Cy polymer) for reduction/pH dual responsive gatekeeper afforded hybrid fluorescent active prodrug nanomaterials which could release the DOX drug by dual stimuli. The optimal prodrugs displayed 220 nm diameter and 22.99% DOX quantity which very slowly released 68.98% DOX into simulated tumor cells in 3 days and showed 7.58% initial drug leak. The CDs-Hyd-DOX released DOX in response to acidic pH and reduction dual triggers through degrading hyperbranched polymeric chains and after that CDs-Hyd revealed a strong fluorescence peak which verified its capacity for usage in cancer theranostics.

Recently, some conjugates of PTX (paclitaxel) with AuNPs were prepared as nanomedicines and effect of drug location on adjusting in vivo and in vitro characteristics were explored [295]. TA-PTX=N–NH-PEG and TA-PEG–NH–N=PTX were achieved and conjugated as PTX therapeutics onto AuNPs surface in diverse places (over conjugate surface or between 1000 Da PEG and AuNPs. The PTX-PEG@AuNPs in which the PTX was positioned between PEG and AuNPs demonstrated greater stability, biocompatibility and water dissolution. Furthermore, an acid responsive hydrazone link was introduced between PEG and PTX in two conjugates for PEG-PTX and PTX release, respectively, to the tumor cells. The PTX release from PEG-PTX happened via esterase hydrolyzed the ester bond formed between PEG and PTX. Hence, the PTX-PEG@AuNPs conjugates indicating a two-step sustained PTX release were appropriate therapeutic materials of boosted anticancer efficiency and less systemic cytotoxicity for Heps bearing animals.

Biocompatible MSNs conjugates were developed indicating suitable diameters and large drug loading efficacy [296]. Tri-ethanolamine mainly adjusted the solution pH and regulated nanomaterial size through formation of complexes by orthosilicate fragment. Besides, extracting micelle template was done using NH4NO3 as the weak acid as well as NaCl for comparison. NH4NO3 created 3.2 nm pore size and 563 m2/g large specific surface area that were greater compared to the values measured using NaCl. The MSNs surface was modified by (3-aminopropyl) tri-ethoxysilane as the amino functional group and a crosslinker for trastuzumab functionalization of MSNs nanostructures that were then encapsulated the DOX medicine to examine targeting breast cancer treatment. Additionally, the surface of functionalized MSNs nanomaterials was further modified by fluorescein dye. Fluorescence images were achieved to approve binding of functionalized MSNs onto plasma membranes of HER2 overexpressing breast SKBR3 sarcoma cells. The functionalized MSNs exhibited 57.40% DOX loading efficiency which verified its capacity for drug delivery. As well, quantum chemical computations using B3LYP/6–311++G** method justified the pH sensitive DOX release from the functionalized MSNs that confirmed data obtained from experiments.

Partial least squares (PLS) regression along with spectroscopic Fourier-transform infrared (PLS/FT-IR) images were used to explore cellular responses to AuNPs-drug conjugate [297]. Indeed, such conjugate effectively differentiated malignant, slightly malignant and non-tumorigenic cells in addition to proposing a method of defining cancer cells sensitiveness towards drug molecules. Diverse concentrations of AuNPs (10, 15 mg/L) plus AuNPs (10, 15 mg/L)/α-methyl-DL-tryptophan (3.75 × 10−6 M) conjugates were applied on different human breast cells including MDA-MB-231 (malignant, pleural effusion, metastatic), MCF7 (mildly malignant, pleural effusion, metastatic) as well as non-tumorigenic MCF10A. Results obtained from the PLS method excellently distinguished the malignant, mildly malignant and non-tumorigenic cells. Additionally, the PLS/FT-IR process showed that differences of control cells and those exposed to AuNPs-drug conjugate were related to their diverse lipid, DNA/RNA, and amide II and I values. AuNPs-drug conjugate affected nucleic acids of all cell categories. Thus, MCF-7 cells indicated a particular response onto such drug therapy and caused interaction of nucleic acids with drug as well as DNA conformational variations.

T-cell dual affinity re-targeting or TCDART conjugates are created by covalently linkage of two diverse single-chain antibodies so that one chain can recognize the antigen over T lymphocytes and other chain can recognize surface antigen over target cells. TCDART conjugates pursue formation of cytolytic synapses between T cells and target cells, which thereby result in lysis and perforation of target cell membrane, or target cells apoptosis promoted via enzymes or T-cell cytokines [298]. It is noteworthy that T cells are one of the essential white blood cells existing in the immune system which exhibit a fundamental influence on adaptive immune responses. T cells are identified from other lymphocytes through the occurrence of T-cell receptors over their cell surfaces. T cells are produced by hematopoietic stem cells existing in bone marrow and then they migrate to the thymus gland and become matured. The origin of T cells name is derived from the thymus organ where they grow and mature.

Herein, a comprehensive study was accomplished on big family of micro- and nanomaterials as DDSs from inorganic nanomaterials to polymeric nanocomposites and stimuli-responsive drug carriers as well as drug conjugates for biomedical applications. The inorganic nanomaterials included carbon nanomaterials (graphene, carbon nanotubes, fullerene derivatives and carbon quantum dots), non-metals, metals and metal oxides as well as porous materials (zeolites, MOFs, MCMs and SBAs). The polymeric nanocomposites were natural biopolymers, synthetic polymers and dendrimers and proteins, peptides, phospholipids, micelles, liposomes and DNA. Numerous smart drug carriers have been designed and developed such as systems that are responsive to pH, temperature, light, magnetic field, enzyme, electrochemical potential glucose, microwave, ultrasonic and ion. It was shown that application of controlled drug delivery vehicles led to developing oral, transdermal and injectable sustained release materials by formulating zero-order carriers, micro- and nano-scales along with self-regulated drug delivery platforms. Also, this field is rapidly growing and leads to the fabrication of plentiful new carriers for controlled delivery/release of drugs like microneedles, micro-reservoir implants, nanoparticles, transdermal patches and antibody drug conjugates. Smart and controlled DDSs enhance the administration, efficacy and pharmacokinetics of therapeutics like enzymes, vaccines, peptides and small molecule drugs. They can enhance the therapeutics bioavailability through higher uptake, non-premature degradation, keeping drugs at their therapeutic windows and diminishing side effects via targeting drugs to specific sites. The controlled drug release with the desired amount would lead to the patients to take on-demand and reproducible dosing when necessary. Certainly, these systems cause exact dosage regulation with desired effects, minimalize side effects, avoid numerous drug administration and device implantation and eventually increase the patients’ conformity.
